# Thread: Type 1 Diabetes x2!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 71
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jaime C — 2025-09-11T07:27:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-359370>

Hi All, I am a mama on a mission to help my two sons (ages 15 and 13) heal from type 1 diabetes. Jaxon (15) was diagnosed almost 2 1/2 years ago and I have been asking questions ever since. Tucker (13) was diagnosed almost 1 year ago; felt like I was re-living the same nightmare from when his bro was diagnosed! They were both admitted/treated at Children's Hospital of Denver. We live on the western slope of CO and are now planning/preparing for a lifestyle change at lower latitude. I have been researching/studying/reading/listening to anything I can get a hold of, as this disease has made no sense to me since day 1! Through my mad hunt for answers, I found Dr. Kruse and have ingested as much as possible over the last couple of months. I've found so many answers, yet have so many questions. I journaled the other day "the more I learn, the less I know". I am humble but tenacious, and I know there is healing for these young men! I'm not even sure this is the right spot for these thoughts, but I'm not getting a response from TeamKruse (or an appointment option for my consultation I paid for last month), so here goes... Thanks for reading; happy to be among a decentralized crowd!

### Jaime C — 2025-09-16T07:29:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-359577>

I'm not finding much direction in the way of what this 'Optimal Journal' should be. So I thought I'd go ahead and write a little more:

Our family in general: my husband and I got married in 2001 and have always owned our own small businesses; mostly in the trades and in real estate. We've homeschooled since our first child was born and our kids are not vaccinated. We've lived in the same rural area since we met - a small town on the western slope of Colorado. My husband is a CO native. I'm kind of from all over the place - but have been here for 25 years now. We have 4 acres of our own, and we live on a preserve of over 100+ shared acres (among our 4 neighbors) so I like to think we have enough open space, fresh air and clean water (we have our own well).

I come from a lineage of what I would consider somewhat un-healthy females, although all in all, feel like I was personally handed fairly good genes (or the ability to overcome some shortfalls somehow). My grandmother was always overweight, super grumpy, and had very thin hair; at least this is what I remember of her. She passed away suddenly (heart attack I believe?) in her 70s. My mother has always struggled with her health, and continues to do so. In high school, she had such a hard time with digestion and GI issues, they did 'exploratory surgery' back in the early 70s. Nothing came of that and she's struggled with weight management, digestive issues and mood/hormone imbalances since I can remember. She is a self-proclaimed researcher (although mostly centralized-based because that's all she's aware of), and has gone down the rabbit holes of iodine/thyroid, DHEA, mold toxicity and everything in between. She still hasn't found the 'magic pill' she's been searching for. I'm convinced she has blue light toxicity and has spent too much time in nnEMF for sure. I feel like I somehow escaped many of her and her mom's issues...

I've always been a healthy weight, had good energy, feel like I can think for myself, support a busy husband and raise a large family. I've never had a diagnosis and I credit my husband for taking such good care of me. I've had a pretty low-stress life being able to stay at home vs. an intense corporate career.

Like I said, my husband and I have been married for 24 years now. Our first son was born in 2003, with seemingly no health issues. He is very athletic and participated in extreme sports like downhill mountain biking, boarder cross (snowboarding) and snowmobiling. Because of injuries in those sports, he has had several concussions. He was recently experiencing (what we think was) post concussion symptoms triggered by recent mild concussion (inability to focus, crazy mood swings, impatience, headaches). He had imaging done to rule out a brain bleed. Thankfully, no brain bleed but there was an incidental finding of a Mega Cisertna Magna of over a cubic 1-2 inches in size. He was told this was inconsequential and wouldn't be what was causing any of his symptoms. He's always been, and continues to be, a very brilliant high-functioning/high-capacity person. He is married and they have a new baby boy, 5 months old (precious!). They live here in the same town we do. Our second child was born in 2004, a daughter. Other than a torn labrum in her hip from sports injuries and a few concussions from mountain biking and softball, she's super healthy as well. She currently lives in Hawaii and is thriving.

On to the reason(s) I am here... our two boys, Jaxon (15 yo) and Tucker (13 yo). They are the youngest of our 4 children and were born and raised here in CO like their siblings. These two were born C-section, while the older two had natural deliveries. The reason for the 3rd child being born C-section was that he was full breach at 37 weeks gestation. I was too young/uneducated/scared to consider any other options for delivery, so C-section it was. When I was pregnant with baby #4, it was assumed he would need to be delivered via C-section as well because our local hospital and facilities consider a VBAC too risky. They are both so similar in some ways, yet polar opposites in others. Here is the story on them:

Jaxon: diagnosed with T1D June of 2023 shortly after his 13th birthday. I was told he was about 50% into DKA. He had lost 10 lbs in about 2 weeks and we had no clue what was happening! Thankfully we caught it in time. He spent a few days at Children's in Denver and we were sent home with a new diagnosis we knew nothing about. Jaxon was on the typical insulin regime for 9 months and we found that a low carb diet was immensely helpful (against his endocrinologist's high-carb recommendation of course). At the 9 month mark, we found Dr. Zofia Clemens out of Hungary who taught us about the Paleolithic Ketogenic Diet, and Jaxon was completely off insulin within 1 week under her care. He remained in ketosis and insulin-free for 13 months. We were hoping this period of time would serve as a reset of sorts, allowing his body to heal to a point of being able to re-introduce foods eventually. That didn't prove to be the case. The PKD is pretty difficult if you can imagine, for a teenager to adhere to (think a high fat, meat only diet).

Tucker: During that 13 months of PKD, Jaxon's younger brother, Tucker was diagnosed with T1D as well. It was the same nightmare-ish situation all over again, although we caught it much earlier because of our unfortunate experience with Jaxon. This all happened in October of 2024, shortly after his 12th birthday. Tucker joined the PKD club and was thankful he didn't have to give injections in the beginning and was able to avoid insulin altogether for a while. After a handful of months though, the diet was not sustainable for Tucker, and he and his brother both agreed to expand their food choices while using minimal insulin (still focusing on low-carb/high fat in a reasonable way).

Currently, they only use 1 dose of long-lasting insulin every 24 hours (16 units each currently). Based on their labs from November of 2024, they both have an adequate C-Peptide reading, showing they are still in the 'honeymoon' phase with some Beta cell function. I believe this (and a low-carb diet) is why they can get by with just their 1 dose per day. Neither of them ever wanted a pump or a CGM. It's like they know they were never meant to have this disease and we are all unable to fully embrace the diagnosis. I know there is a way for them to heal from this!

I'm not sure what else to say specifically about these boys. They are active CO boys who enjoy the mountain life style. They are sporty, smart, hard-working, kind and funny. There are few things in their world that I can attribute their T1D diagnosis to, other than we recently discovered high levels of radon in our home. I've been wanting to ask Dr. Kruse what his take on radon exposure is, as I can't seem to find anything in the blogs about this. Our first reading from a radon meter was 22+. Thankfully we mitigated immediately and our readings now fluctuate between .5-1.5. However, from what I've researched it doesn't seem like there's much recovery from toxic levels of radon. I'd love to hear that there is!

At the moment, we have plans to leave CO and join our daughter in HI from Thanksgiving through the New Year. That would give us a solid 6 weeks to adopt a lifestyle that revolves around circadian rhythm, grounding, and lots of accessible sea food! Of course, there's the volcanic piece there too, the lower latitude and the favorable temps where the boys could be outdoors all/most of the day. I'm looking forward to giving them this opportunity for healing, and would love to hear if there are any others who have reversed T1D by adopting this lifestyle. I'm so curious to know their story... how long did it take? Anything specific or out of the ordinary that needs to be done? Or just watch the sunrise, eat a BAB every morning, surf all day and maybe visit some volcanos? Is it that simple?

I'd love any thoughts and feedback from anyone out there who has T1D experience specifically. Or, just general insights on any of the above. Thanks for reading!

### Type1Carnivore — 2025-09-16T09:41:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-359586>

Hello,
sorry to hear your kids have T1D. I myself have had T1D since 2009. Ofcourse I didnt know about low carb or carnivore diets back then. So after i was diagnosed I was on a SAD for years. With pretty bad sugar control(rollarcoster sugars). A1C's ranging between 5.9-6.3 usually. Been looking for ways to improve my condition as well but since my beta cells production is completely gone I have not found a way to completely reverse it. Low carb diets help a lot(im carnivore myself(last 15 months and my last A1c's have been 5.2). Bernsteins book is pretty good, dunno if you have read it. Ryan Attar has some good content as well(check youtube). I've been incorporating some of Kruses stuff. Way more sun, better circadian rhythm(sunrise,sunset), blue blocking glasses, trying to limit nnemf exposure, as much grounding as possible etc.(within whats possible for my living conditions).

I still get fluctuating sugars usually ranging between 70-120. Its the best I havee been able to get. Its hard to predict how much glucagon your alpha cells release since your body doesn't counter it with its own insulin.

I've been looking for years to find people who have completely reserved T1D. Still have not found any.

### Jaime C — 2025-09-16T15:56:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-359596>

Thank you for sharing your story! I am sorry to hear you battle similar things; this disease seems to be a tricky, awful one! I have read Berstein's book several times (ordered that before we left the hospital!) and yes, it's super helpful. Kudos to you for doing all the Kruse things! I'm sure they all help. I wish I could offer some advice to you, but you are the veteran here.

Interesting you mentioned alpha/glucagon and beta/insulin. I've been so curious about this balance, as well as the liver releasing glucose too. Sometimes I wonder if T1D can be more of an issue of too much glucagon and glucose and maybe a little less of a beta/insulin issue. The docs never mention the fact that our bodies have their own natural way to run on sugars if needed - they only tell you there aren't enough beta cells. Maybe that's true (that it's only a beta cell thing) but the body is so beautifully complex, I feel like there is more to uncover.

Thank you for the other tips and resources as well! I believe I have exhausted all rabbit holes that I have been able to seek out - followed them each to their end until I find myself here! I appreciate the search fields in the forums and blogs, and I have spent much time reading and chasing down all things T1D, diabetes in general, and autoimmune here with Kruse's work. I've read Becker's book, epi-paleo Rx, listened to every podcast I can find Dr. Kruse on am catching up on backlogs of Q&A sessions. There is a Q&A from Oct 2016 where someone asks Dr. Kruse about reversing T1D and he confidently, repeatedly says 'yes, yes....'. This gives me hope but I want to see if there is anything specific to know before heading to HI. Figured a consult would be a best next step to see if there is anything else.

Best wishes to you and your health journey!

### Jaime C — 2025-09-16T16:01:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-359597>

Other thoughts and details I had after writing the journal entry last night, so I'll add them here...

Tucker (the 13 yo) was diagnosed with Celiac disease at age 5. My grandmother (mom's mom) also had celiac. My mom's sister had celiac, T1D and died of lung cancer in her late 40s.

Both of my pregnancies with them were happily uneventful and healthy (at least I had no reason to think they weren't)

We'll be on the Big Island, staying in rural areas. I have visions of the boys spearfishing, boogie boarding and getting their schoolwork done outdoors under the shade of trees. I'm looking forward to a more favorable climate to do all the things in (sunrise, grounding, etc.). It's 34 degrees here in the mornings now and our days are getting shorter. We live in arguably one of the coldest spots in the nation.

Thinking back to our habits around lights and screens, there was plenty of room for improvement (had I known the dangers and effects!). Happy to be in the know now, and to be working towards those changes.

### MTA321 — 2025-09-17T17:40:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-359649>

I think I remember seeing a meta-analysis about children being born via cesarean section and having a 15-20% higher risk of developing T1D. Possibly points to early contributors such as an altered microbiome exposure via lack of vaginal birth among others. Again, I am sure there are other factors (maternal germline, heteroplasmy rate, light environment, etc.) at play regardless. Just interesting that both c-section babies developed T1D.

Ha! Seems to 'fit'! That is actually the very first article that I came across of yours a few months ago. It's been an adventure searching for all the other nuggets on T1D on your blogs/forums/podcasts, etc.

Thank you for your work, Dr. Kruse.

And yes - I get it, we are heading south.

If you're willing to go into details here, I'd love to know if there would be any specific protocols you'd recommend for us? Please understand, I have read Epi-Paleo Rx, and have become very familiar with your work in this area. I understand the basics, even if I can't quite speak the POMC-momcie language yet . Specifically, I'm wondering if there is anything different I should consider doing for these boys while we are in HI besides what I would consider your standard of care, which would include sunlight, grounding, seafood, clean water, avoid nnEMF / blue light...

For example:

Since we'll be on the Big Island, would there be any benefit in spending more time near the actual volcanic activity?

In your opinion, what would be a good time frame to give this 'our best shot' to see if we can move the needle for their condition? 6 weeks is tangible/doable for our family at this point. I understand you can't guarantee or promise anything (or maybe you can?!), but do you think that's enough time to start seeing a change in their own insulin production? Of course, if the change does start happening in that time frame, we will do what we must to keep them there longer.

I understand n=1; what considerations would you suggest in my situation where it would be very easy for me to otherwise lump these brothers together like an n=2 in their treatment? What could be different for each of them and/or what should I keep an eye out for when viewing them each as being on their own healing journeys?

If this is too detailed, please know I've already paid for a consult and am waiting to get on your calendar. Looking forward to getting more individual guidance.

PS As much as I so badly want healing for my boys, I am equally motivated in this to prove the world wrong in their fear-based mentality and limited thinking. Someone has to do it, and I'm willing to take the leap and change the T1D story for the sake of future generations.

Most people with T1D won't move to the sun to get the immunosuppressive affect of sunlight to gain photorepair. You need to turn off the pathological T regulator cells that cause this disease as the first step of rebuilding the AMO physics inside the cell. Once that is done then you need UVa/IRA/NIR to fix the AMO physics broken and voila........you can get better. Few have the discipline of the patience to do it. IT happens on the cellular timeline not youre own. One person on this forum showed that staying power. Neurotic Farmer had a disease she thought was uncurable and after 7-10 yrs it reversed and it stunned her.

When I see a T1D first thing I do is make them get naked and look at their melanin status externally before I look inside.

Every disease is reversable, not every patient is.

### Jaime C — 2025-09-25T03:01:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-360020>

Since starting this journal 2 weeks ago, the boys' insulin needs have increased significantly. I'm sure it's because they aren't outside as much, it's cold, and who knows what else!? Two teenagers entering/going through puberty isn't the most stable situation for hormones!

I have my hopes up for Hawaii. However, after reading other's struggles even after changing their environment, I have to say it's hard to put all my eggs in this one basket. I need more detailed info on what it takes to reverse T1D specifically. I don't feel like I have enough data on them individually, in their n=1, but Idk what I would even look for. How do I determine what their n=1 is? I need a specific plan in order to have more confidence going into this. And they need to feel secure in that plan as well, so they have motivation.

I'm trying to put this HI plan together, yet I don't have any guarantees, so I feel conflicted. But I don't necessarily have any other bright ideas that we haven't already tried, so we will go for it. We'll go for it whole-heartedly and do our very best.

I have to admit though, it would be a little easier to take this leap of faith if there was more than just one other success story from almost 2 decades ago. I can't seem to find any additional info on that person's story either - like, what did he/she do specifically, or was it the general Leptin Rx, E-P Rx, etc. that was enough? I've learned so much, yet have so much more to understand. But, time is ticking! I fear their 'honeymoon' phase is ending for them, and that regaining their own insulin production will be exponentially harder because of this.

I suppose if nothing else, this journal serves as a spot for my thoughts.

### Jaime C — 2026-01-03T09:30:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-364507>

Just arrived home today after 6 weeks in the Hawaiian Islands. It was magical, helpful for the boys, great for our family, and it makes me want to move to the tropics! For other T1D families who may be interested, we saw some pretty remarkable improvements in their health and a decrease in insulin dosages overall, yet the 6-week span was somewhat of a rollercoaster. This is what we did:

Up for breakfast at sunrise every morning. Most days, I chose to get up an hour early to cook and pack food in the truck so we could watch sunrise at a beach with our picnic breakfast. This worked well to keep all of us outside for hours in the mornings. I noticed if we stayed at the house for sunrise and breakfast, the boys would inevitably wander back indoors for this or that. Being away from the house helped with my plan of being the sunshine police! We'd spend 80% of the day in the sunshine barefoot, wrapping it up with a sunset dinner each night. Sometimes during the day, we'd use an umbrella or sit under trees for shade, but since they got the morning sunlight, even we Colorado white people tanned pretty darn well! We are all a little more blonde, no one burnt, and of course we didn't use sunscreen or sunglasses at all.

After dinner, it was only candlelight. We'd play card games outside with taper candles if the boys weren't worn out enough for bed by dark. When we first arrived at the house, I unplugged the TVs and other small appliances. I was mindful to run the dishwasher, washer and dryer on our way out the door in the morning so those weren't running at night while we were sleeping. The house had a shared Starlink setup with a neighbor, so that was unavoidable for our situation. The boys did watch a couple 20-min YouTube sessions on my laptop outside during the day so they could get some tips and tricks on spearfishing and skim boarding, but I was pretty much a screen nazi. I even had them give me their devices and I powered them off the whole time we were there.

For about 3 1/2 weeks of the 6-week span, they drank 10ppm DDW strictly. Shipping those cases of water to HI takes a while and is super expensive, so considering logistics, this was our best efforts on giving them as much as possible. If/when we can return to the tropics, I would plan it out even better and make sure they could have it for the full stay if we can make that work! Looking back, I think that correlated with their decrease insulin usage although there were quite a few variables so who knows (activity level, sleep quality, did we drive to Kona that day for groceries or not?...). When they didn't have DDW, they drank bottled distilled water from the store. Our rental house had a UV-treated water catchment system with an additional filtration system but I didn't trust it as drinking water for any of us.

We had some awesome adventures - surfing, snorkeling, swimming with dolphins, visiting volcanos, sailing lessons, body boarding, spearfishing, skim boarding, football on the beach, visiting with my daughter who lives there, great family time and a little bit of bookwork on the deck to keep up with homeschooling. We ate a serving of local seafood pretty much every day. Mostly Ahi, since we had a personal connection with a local fisherman there. We also made a lot of local grass fed beef hamburgers, with salads made with local produce. Breakfasts were local pork breakfast sausage or bacon (not necessarily local) and sometimes a locally-grown sweet potato diced and cooked in coconut oil, with the best Hawaiian sea salt!

Altogether, their blood sugar levels would fluctuate - mostly for the good. At one point they were both using less than half the amount of insulin they were using at home! At this point today (after long flights, airports, exhaustion, dehydration, sadness from leaving, haha!), they are about at the same dosage they were when we left. I'd hoped to see more drastic, linear, fast progress because I am an optimist at heart. To be fair, Dr. Kruse said it would take 3-6 months before I could even tell if this was going to work for them or not. I've seen enough response in their bodies to have plenty of hope this would work if we can make a long-term solution work for our family.

Other symptoms that completely went away for the boys: headaches, bloody noses, anxiety and constipation. they didn't always sleep the best and of course that could be a number of different things, but I'd hoped that would have stayed consistent since they do actually sleep really well at home.

It was so wonderful to be able to eat locally-sourced, fresh foods; which provided way more variety than what's available here in CO at the moment (which is not much more than meat and fat from the freezer). We are low-carb/keto because it helps us manage their blood sugars more easily anyways, but it sure was wonderful to give them fresh avocados, cook in coconut oil and give them local coconut yogurt. It's back to beef roasts and meats cooked in tallow for now!

I'm feeling encouraged in my hope for their healing. As their mama, I feel confident that I did the best for our situation at this point in time. The protocol is not complicated, but it's wasn't just a 'Hawaiian Vacation' either. Our intentions were very focused. It was hard work, rewarding, and I hope to do it all over again in whatever capacity our family can make happen!

P.S. Dr. Kruse, if you're reading... one question I have for you: What would the ideal schedule of sunlight be for T1D reversal? During our consult, you said basically 'all the sun, all the time' which is what we did... but I just read in a recent comment section of one of your posts where you mentioned even the protocol for eczema vs psoriasis would be different. What direction would you give to me if/when I were to be able to do this again (either in HI or possibly ES now that we have their passports in hand)?

P.S.S. I've seen hints on the forum of your upcoming short term housing developments/opportunities that you are putting together in ES but Idk how to see those options specifically. If you think one of your developments would be a good fit for me and the boys (and hubby when he can come between business commitments), could you please point me in the right direction? I'd love to explore options.

Thanks All!

Anything that blocks ETC from Cytochrome 2-4 causes reverse electron flow to cytochrome 1 and ROS rises and UPEs made = diabetes.

Statins, no melanin = no Cu control in the matrix which blocks CCO since it is co factor. Statins blocks cytochrome 3 to cause same issue. Cu is needed for melanin creation.

Cyanobacterial O2 surge created "dirty" chemistry where ROS spikes and bioavailable transition metals (Fe, Cu, Mn, Mo and deuterium) fueling Fenton reactions. Melanin evolved for enhanced transition metal enhanced chelation to control these metals for precision mitochondrial function, and binding metals helped to buffer ROS that makes non coherent UPEs causing diabetic phenotype issues. Without a control knob for ROS, diabetics lose their superoxide burst.

This optimized aerobic metabolism for Earth with a 21% oxygen level, allowing eukaryotic complexity amid oxidative bottlenecks; melanin acted as an "electronic sink", and as such, allowed life to bridge Earth's anaerobic origins to aerobic an world in the GOE. Today, modern life tech abuse causes hypoxia and low water production at CCO which mimics diabetes.

Primates to humans: Surface melanin-producing melanocytes migrated inward (via SOX10/WNT/EDN3 signaling) to sculpt tissues, via internal pigmentation for radiosynthesis to charge transition metals for mitochondrial metabolism, metal chelation, splitting of water, thermoregulation, camouflage, and optical electronic signaling in rebuilding ecosystems. Endogenous melanin thus internalized for complexity, enabling vertebrate diversification amid post-extinction niches. Diabetes is a step back to the GOE.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-02-07T20:42:40+0200

What triad to diabetics get related to the leptin melanocortin pathway loss?
Polydipsia, polyuria, and polyphagia. Why?

The hormone controlling human water balance, vasopressin, is usually released at night when light MUST BE FULLY absent. If it is not absent the next day cortisol from POMC will be on the ground and you will feel like you got hit by a truck.

Artificial light post-sunset causes massive early chronic release of vasopressin. Screen time during the day exacerbates this. Blue light causes light stress during the day when vasopressin should not be active.

So, a blue light hazard (polarization) mimics a TBI = hypoxia/disrupted CCO = water production. This is why I tell my tribe that MS is another disease that is a TBI due to screen abuse and a lack of sun because no one using technology at a high rate is outside. They are under indoor blue light made by man. To repair MS myelin defects requires immunosuppression of the T Regulator cells that ruined the AMO physics inside of a cell that creates myelin. the leptin melanocortin pathways controls that biology because you need to use the TCA cycle to make myelin.

Once that UV light stops the immune prioduction of damage then the normal visible spectrum of UVA, IRA, and NIR restores the default pathways of the TCA/urea cycles need to remyelinate. One cannot use these pathways unless one senses AM sunrise. AM sunrise also raises cortisol and this allows us to begin the photorepair process. This is critical in developing many diseases like Multiple Sclerosis. Research has shown that people with MS respond to vasopressin antagonist drugs to heal their myelin deficits. This tells clinicians that the chronic release of vasopressin to light stress is a critical problem in this immune-mediated disease. It has ZERO to do with the food you eat. The same is true of diabetes.

Implications of my comments above?

Epigenetic Planning and the Parent-Child Link

This is perhaps my most critical point: **The lack of POMC/Melanin translation in the parents germ line cells is "pre-loading" the embryo for failure.

Information vs. Energy:** If the parental germline (sperm/egg) is "deuterium-loaded" or biophotonically "dim" due to a lack of melanin/POMC regulation, the **initial condition** of the embryo is thermodynamically compromised. How T1D are created.

**The Default Program:** The embryo doesn't have the energy to "unfold" the complex mammalian brain. It defaults to the "more stable genetic package" (the older phylogeny). Childhood diabetes is a step back in time.

**The Non-Genetic Epidemic:** This explains why childhood diseases (autism, childhood cancers, sensory processing disorders) are exploding despite no change in the DNA sequence. It’s an **energy-transformation deficit** due to a lack of POMc/melanin in key tissues that forces the child’s biology to "reach back" to a more primitive, ruggedized (but maladapted) evolutionary state.

How does diabetes always begin? It creates a mismatch in the eye clock and this destroys all the peripheral clocks it relies on.

Before you add another supplement, recovery tool, or protocol,
it’s worth asking whether your environment is giving your biology the right signals.

Most persistent health problems aren’t personal failures.

They’re photonic signal problems that lead to electronic errors.

This is for high-performing adults everywhere who are doing the obvious things right, training consistently, eating well, managing stress, and still feel plateaued, frustrated, or quietly exhausted.

That experience isn’t random.

It’s what happens when symptoms are addressed at the biochemistry level while the biophysical environment shaping those systems goes completely unexamined by ignorant centralized MDs

Over a very short period of time, human life moved indoors.
Work did.
Training did.
Recovery did.

Your biology didn’t evolve for that shift on this timescale.

The common mistake is trying to fix downstream outputs without stabilizing the upstream signals that regulate them.

Your body works in this order:
Physics (light, dark, timing, temperature)
Energy production (mitochondria)
Gene expression (epigenetics)
Chemistry (biochemistry)
Symptoms

You can’t reliably change chemistry or symptoms without respecting physics first.

That’s not ideology.

That’s quantum biology of the circadian mechanism of man.

If you’re like my clients in globally, you’ve tried everything and still feel confused, plateaued, or worn down, even though your effort keeps increasing.

That’s what a signal mismatch feels like.

Correcting upstream signals isn’t passive, it requires active actions for remedy.

It requires participation and consistency, not because you need to try harder, but because biology responds to what it’s exposed to.

When the environment begins to make sense to the system, morphology changes, biocheical patterns change in cells.

Energy delievery becomes more reliable.
Information becomes less subject to alteration.
Recovery becomes more predictable.
Training stops feeling like diminishing returns.
Not through force,
through alignment
Via TIMING.

The SCN is known as the"Master Clock" that dictates the very ability of a mammal to function in time and space. The SCN needs an unwavering, direct link to ambient light to entrain the entire organism's circadian rhythm, pupillary response, and autonomic functions (cranial nerve III).
Melanopsin was the Ancient Adaptability Engine put into the visual system to drive the clock mechanism: This system allows the mammal to anticipate dawn/dusk cycles, thermal shifts, and UV index changes, making the organism highly adaptable to any environmental change.

When this protected, ancient opsin system that controls the morphology of the central retinal pathways is disrupted by modern polarized light (through chronic artificial light exposure or specific SCN lesions), the result is profound:

Humans Lose Adaptability: Without a stable, internally generated "Time Crystal" signal from the SCN/Melanopsin axis, the mammal cannot synchronize its internal chemistry with external physics.

The Atavistic Behavioral Collapse: The organism can no longer manage its Deuterium/Melanin/Metal chemistry effectively to control its own mitochondrial biochemistry. Diabetes is a problem where the most complex eukaryote is forced to use glucose because the TCA and urea cycle are not available to them. This is what simply life forms had to do in the GOE.

The stress response (HPA axis/ACTH) becomes chaotic, leading to the "loner" social behavior, chronic stress, and systemic inflammation we see in modern chronic diseases and mental illness (ADHD/BPD/Depressions/Schizophrenia). All these disease more common in diabetics.

The melanopsin system is the ultimate evolutionary safeguard of the human blockchain, a robust, simple optical electronic "master switch" that ensures the organism at least knows when it is, even if it can't tell you what it is looking at. Without it you are subject to injury, psy-ops, and disease.

This work follows a simple hierarchy:
Environment → Energy → Capacity
Stabilize light by stabilizing timing.

Support mitochondrial signaling.

By realizing light is upstream all things in this system.

Let biochemistry and symptoms resolve downstream.

There’s no passive option, but there is clarity.

If this way of thinking reframes problems you’ve been stuck on, a strategy session or the whitepaper may be useful next steps.

If you’re looking for convenience over understanding, this probably isn’t the right lane for you, and that’s my intention.

I'm not for everyone.

Not a bug, but my design.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-03-06T15:54:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-368256>

The same thing. When you cannot repair your non visual photoreceptors because melantonin, dopamine, and GABA are dysregulated the dopamine receptors change and lead to your behavioral change due to D2 receptor changes.

This should be a profound realization for you.

What I'm describing here is that "addictive" pull of the sun, is not just a metaphor; it is a validated biological feedback loop involving the
**POMC (Pro-opiomelanocortin)** pathway. When your skin is exposed to UVB, the body doesn't just produce Vitamin D. It cleaves the POMC protein into three main components: Alpha-MSH (which creates a tan), **ACTH** (which regulates stress), and beta-endorphin. You are quite literally "getting high" on endogenous opioids produced by your skin.

For a recovering alcoholic, this is a "clean" version of the same dopaminergic/opioid circuit you spent 17 years recalibrating.

**1\. The Shared Pathology: Reward Deficiency Syndrome**
In my thesis, you can argue that both **Diabetes** and **Alcoholism** are symptoms of **Reward Deficiency Syndrome (RDS)**, driven by the downregulation of **D2 dopamine receptors**.

**The Diabetic:** Low dopamine in the brain's reward centers leads to "sugar-seeking" behavior. The body tries to use glucose to spike dopamine when the solar "tonic" signal is missing.

**The Alcoholic:** Uses ethanol to artificially force a dopamine release that the environment (lack of UV/IR light) is failing to provide.

**The Solar Connection:** Sunlight (specifically UV-A and IR-A, as shown in my image) is the natural regulator of retinal and systemic dopamine. Without it, the D2 receptors "starve," and the human seeks a chemical proxy (sugar or alcohol) for the missing light.

**2\. The Latitude Curve: Russians, the Irish, and the "Solar Debt"**
Your observation about the latitude curve is biologically sound. In high-latitude regions (Ireland, Russia, Scandinavia), there is a chronic **"UV-B deficit"** for much of the year.

**The Irish/Russian Context:** If you cannot produce beta-endorphin or dopamine through the skin and eyes via UV light, the culture often "self-medicates" with alcohol to reach the same neurochemical baseline.

**The Mitochondrial Link:** Alcohol and high blood sugar both increase **heteroplasmy** and mitochondrial swelling, the exact same damage I identified in the **Blau Lab** study I put in the chromosome #2 thread on here. This creates a vicious cycle: low light
→
low dopamine
→
chemical consumption
→
mitochondrial damage
→
further loss of regenerative capacity.

**3\. The Retinal Triad: Melatonin, Dopamine, and GABA**
The image above is the key to the "behavioral lock." It shows that **Melatonin, Dopamine, and GABA** are not separate systems; they are a synchronized "clock" in the retina.

-   **Dopamine (D1/D2):** The "Light" is the signal. It tells the brain it is day and to be active.
-   **Melatonin:** The "Dark" signal. It regenerates the photoreceptors and resets the mitochondria.
-   **The Dysregulation:**If you lack UV/IR light (as shown at the top of the image), you cannot regenerate the photoreceptors. This means:
    1.  **Low Dopamine:** Leading to the "addiction" or "hunger" for a chemical spike.
    2.  **Low GABA:** Leading to the "anxiety" and "one-more-drink/one-more-donut" compulsion.
    3.  **Inversion:** You end up with a "broken clock" where the body is stuck in a state of high-stress/low-repair.

**4\. How this fits the GLP-1 Thesis**
GLP-1 drugs (Ozempic) sit right in the middle of this. By forcing a metabolic change without fixing the **dopaminergic/solar deficit**, the drug may be "locking" the patient into a state of **biochemical sobriety but cellular starvation.**
If the drug impairs the stem cells that repair the very tissue (the heart/muscle) being hammered by this dysregulation, you aren't just losing weight; you are accelerating the "time inflation" leading to a loss of longevity via high heteroplasmy.

**The "One more" feeling:** That feeling of "one is too many and a hundred is not enough" is the hallmark of **low D2 receptor density**. Your body is desperately trying to "fill the tank" because the solar-powered pump is broken.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-03-06T16:00:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-368258>

Because your baseline (as a Type 1 diabetic or someone with a history of alcoholism) may involve a "starved" reward system, the sudden influx of solar-mediated neurochemicals isn't just a "nice feeling"—it is a **systemic rescue signal**.

**1\. The "Yearning" is Reward Deficiency Syndrome (RDS)**
You mentioned the alcoholic motto: _"One is too many and a hundred is not enough."_ This is the classic definition of **D2 dopamine receptor deficiency**.

**The Baseline:** If your D2 receptors are low (due to genetics or metabolic disease), your brain exists in a state of "noise" and "hunger." You aren't at zero; you are at a deficit.
**The Hit:** When you hit index 7+ UVB, the POMC cleavage in your skin creates a massive surge of beta endorphins and dopamine.
**The Amplification:** For an "average Joe" with normal receptor density, this is a 10/10 reward. For you, it is the difference between "starving" and "feasting." The _delta_ (the change from baseline to peak) is much larger, which creates that "addictive" pull. Your brain is desperately trying to "lock in" the repair signal while it's available.

**2\. Why Type 1 Diabetics "Yearn" More**
In Type 1 Diabetes, the **dopaminergic-insulin axis** is fundamentally altered.

**Retinal Dopamine:** Research shows that diabetes significantly depletes **retinal dopamine** levels (down to 20–30% in some models). Since the retina is a direct extension of the brain, this depletion is systemic.

**The "Rescue" Effect:** Sunlight is the only natural way to "refill" these retinal dopamine stores. When you sit in the sun, you are literally performing a **neurochemical transfusion**. Your reluctance to "pull yourself out" is your body’s survival mechanism trying to fix the dopaminergic deficit that Ozempic and other drugs ignore.

**3\. Alcoholism and the "Solar Debt"**
The link between alcoholics and solar deficiency is found in the **Latitude Curve** of addiction.

**The Proxy:** Alcohol is a chemical "hack" that forces a dopamine release. People in low-light environments (high latitudes like Ireland or Russia) often use alcohol to achieve the beta endorphin/dopamine state that the sun provides for free at the equator.

**The Withdrawal:** When you quit drinking 17 years ago, you removed the "chemical sun." Now, when you get _actual_ sun, your brain recognizes the original "key" to the lock. The "addiction" you feel is actually a **homeostatic correction**.

**4\. The Role of the "Solar Callus"**
You mentioned that you thought others didn't feel it because they lacked a "solar callus." You are half-right.

**The Filter:** Melanin and the thickening of the stratum corneum (the callus) act as a **semiconductor**. It regulates the _speed_ and _depth_ of the light's penetration.

**The Sensitivity:** Without a callus, people burn and feel "pain," which triggers a cortisol spike that overrides the dopamine reward.

**Your Advantage:** Because you have built the callus, you have filtered out the "noise" (pain/inflammation) and are receiving the **pure signal** (dopamine/endorphins). You have optimized your hardware to receive the "hit" that others are blocking with sunglasses and sunscreen.

The Comparison: Normal vs. "Hungry" Systems

| Feature | Average Person | T1 Diabetic / Recovering Alcoholic |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **D2 Receptor Density** | Normal | **Low (Hypodopaminergic)** |
| **Retinal Dopamine** | Saturated | **Depleted (Chronic Deficit)** |
| **UVB Reward (POMC)** | "Enjoyable" | **"Addictive / Compulsive"** |
| **Biological Intent** | Leisure | **Systemic Repair / Homeostasis** |

You can argue that **addiction is a "misplaced solar search."** Whether it's porn, sugar (diabetes) or ethanol (alcoholism), the human is seeking a dopaminergic "fix" for a biological system that was designed to be powered by the sun. When you provide the _actual_ sun, the "addiction" isn't a problem, it's the **cure**.

Amazing info from Dr. Kruse above, and thanks for sharing part of your story Griff!

Quick update on things here... Jaxon (the 15 year old) has shingles! WTF? Awful, itchy, painful, angry rash that I couldn't figure out. Took him to urgent care to get a diagnosis so we would know how to treat it... poor kid! He was prescribed an anti-viral, which we chose not to fill, and an anti-bacterial ointment for prevention of the sores possibly getting infected. I did get that in case we need it, but the virus seems to be doing it's thing: ugly stage after ugly stage.

Of all places to get shingles... it's on his butt! So sitting, moving around, walking, even standing is uncomfortable. He's laying on his tummy on the lanai with an ice pack on his bum at the moment.

Varicella in our family... none of our four kids were vaccinated for this (or anything/no immunizations). When my husband was 30, he broke out in shingles. At the time, our oldest was 7, he got chicken pox. Our girl was 5, she got chicken pox. Jaxon was two months old, and he got about 10-15 pox total, so a very mild case.

So here we are, doing all the things in the tropics... and this! I'm wondering if the youngest will now break out with Chicken Pox, we'll see...

When I try to research what could trigger this in a teenager, the googles say 'sudden increase in UV exposure can trigger shingles'. Which tracks, but seems like just another message from big harma because it goes against everything I've been learning here. ### Jack Kruse — 2026-03-09T05:29:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-368420>

Shingles often strikes the elderly or the immunosuppressed, those with the highest accumulation of **"zombie cells"** and the lowest **Solar Dopamine**. A shingles outbreak is a signal that the "bioenergetic enclosure" is failing. Shingles is the body's attempt to "exhale" a viral load it can no longer "insulate" with light and grounding.

In **Diabetics** and **Night-Shift workers**, the system enters **Pseudohypoxia,** a state where oxygen is present, but the **NAD+/NADH ratio** is so skewed that the mitochondria cannot "see" it. Without the **220nm VUV internal sun** signal in the RPE-SCN, the tissue looks "electrically dead" to the immune system. In conditions like **Fatal COVID, ARDS, or Sepsis**, sPLA2-IIA levels can spike 100-fold. Because the pseudohypoxia is systemic, sPLA2 doesn't just target one "zombie cell"; it begins to take out **entire organ systems** simultaneously. This is the biological "Supernova" where the transition from a localized "eruption" (acne/eczema) to a systemic "collapse."

If **BCL11A** on **Chromosome 2** is the "Master Rheostat" of blood viscosity and spin-state, its failure is what "invites" sPLA2 to the crime scene.
When the rheostat fails (low solar dopamine/low grounding), the blood becomes a "sludge" of low Zeta Potential.
sPLA2-IIA then "shreds" the mitochondrial membranes of the Peripheral nerves, lung (ARDS) or the vascular endothelium (Sepsis) because they no longer possess the **Melanin/Hydration shield** necessary to maintain their dielectric identity.
Healthy mitochondrial membranes in the nerve root maintain a specific "charge flip" to prevent self-digestion. Without the **Solar Dopamine/Grounding** to maintain this "cloaking" charge, the membrane phospholipids (like cardiolipin) become exposed. sPLA2-IIA recognizes this "electrically dead" signature and hydrolyzes the mitochondrial membranes. This releases a flood of **mtDNA** and **Singlet Oxygen**, which further stimulates the **Cell Danger Response (CDR)**.

A dermatome is a specific "lane" in the body’s particle accelerator.
**The Sludge:** When the blood becomes a "sludge" of low Zeta Potential, the **oxygen delivery** to that specific nerve segment drops (Pseudohypoxia).
**The Eruption:** The nerve can no longer "tunnel" its protons to the ground. The pressure builds until the "volcanic eruption" occurs at the skin surface (Shingles/Eczema).
**The Executioner's Path:** sPLA2-IIA follows this path of **lost coherence**, shredding the myelin and mitochondrial hardware all the way from the spine to the skin.
The agonizing "post-herpetic" pain is the result of the **stagnant mass** left behind in the shingles lesions.
The executioner (sPLA2) has destroyed the "hardware" (mitochondria/membranes) in the nerves.
Because the system is **no longer "costly in time"** (low autophagy/low solar flux), it cannot clear the "debris" of the shredded nerves.
This "debris" continues to broadcast **non-coherent UPEs**, keeping the brain in a state of chronic alarm.
Since BCL11A is the master control for the **viscosity and spin-state** of the blood, then its failure is what turns the "high-speed fiber optic" nerve into a "leaky, sparking wire as the pic above shows." Without the correct spin-state (CISS effect), the electrons cannot tunnel efficiently, and the **dielectric tension** eventually snaps.

Ok Jack this definitely catches my curiosity. I want to give you insight on everything that I’ve been doing since the beginning of the summer of 2025 since I’ve went down all the food holes and realized none of them were going to fix this disease although the meat, eggs , wild game and seafood definitely help blood sugar control I’m starting to see how the too much protein seems to elevate my sugar more than it use too and doesn’t seem as sustainable anymore. I stopped wearing sunglasses last year during spring time and my skin burned that initial time but i peeled and was still dark underneath. After that it brought back memories of when i was a kid / teenager we ran around all summer long barefoot and no shirts and i don’t remember ever getting a sunburn. Beginning July of 2025 we took a trip to Jamaica for a week i stayed in the sun either in the pool or on the beach and i got really dark darker than i could recall when i was younger and i also had a small like blood mole i believe it was on my forehead that use to bleed pretty good if it got scratched, didn’t even realize for about a week or two that it was completely gone. That definitely convinced me about the suns power. Since then i have been trying to listen to every podcast i can find that you have done which was before i became a gold member about a month ago. I’ve been trying to get the am sunlight in my eyes every morning i was actually able to ground myself in my back yard this Saturday and Sunday morning and i was definitely shocked at what i witnessed i only ate eggs and bacon for breakfast and boiled crawfish and oysters both days and i didn’t take the insulin either day. I’m a industrial equipment mechanic by trade so I’m mostly outside during the day for work and mostly ride with the windows down when i can from job to job. I bought a cold plunge tub a few months ago and just finally put it up, I’ve gotten in it twice so far at 50 to 55 degrees i actually enjoy the cold weather i always have i seem to thrive more during the winter time. I live at the 28th latitude in Desallemands Louisiana my question though is the sun where i live strong enough for me here to reverse my T1D?

### Jaime C — 2026-04-13T03:09:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-370827>

Happy Sunday, All! Quick update... lots of beach time and the new family sport/hobby is surfing! I've had small amounts of time here and there to skim recent blogs and try to continue connecting dots. I find myself catching glimpses of different rabbit holes, as well as T1D being specifically mentioned more and more (thank you - I see those notes!). I often scroll back through the last few pages here on this thread to remind myself of all the things - super helpful!

I wish I knew a more detailed recipe for each of my boys' n=1. When I see those rabbit holes (LSD?!) it makes me wonder if what I'm doing is enough, or if each kiddo needs something different or more of something? Or, just time...

On the positive side, both boys are using about half of the insulin they were when we arrived. Moods and spirits are high, and I can sense the dopamine increase in our 13 year old especially.

Timing continues to be king - timing with the sunrise to get up, and going to bed at sunset. I see the effects of staying up late with extra candles just to play more card games together. Not your typical teenager's lifestyle here, lol! Wouldn't have it any other way. These boys are becoming feral yet disciplined.

Continually thankful for the info and opportunity. My husband says someday we'll write a book; I'm not sure about that!

### Jaime C — 2026-04-14T09:27:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-371020>

On the practical, day-to-day side of things here at the beach...

#1 The 15 yo loves surfing so much, he will be in the ocean for hours at a time. His eyes are showing it! Sunburned/bloodshot. I've started having him take breaks and come sit under the umbrella once every hour or two but literally that kid will stay out in the full sun all day on that surfboard if I let him. Seriously though, he looks high all the time now because the whites of his eyes are continually red. This can't be good. Any tips?

#2 Most mornings, we are Dawn Patrol at the beach for a sunrise breakfast. But on the mornings we choose to stay home and eat breakfast outside at the house, our skin doesn't 'see' the sun for about two hours since we are on a steep west-facing slope. It's also cooler at our place in the mornings, so we have more clothes on. I've read before in the posts to 'just be outside' when the sunrise is happening, and 'the light will find you'... is it truly effective enough for my boys in this case, or do we need to head to the beach every single morning, where they can get the sun on their skin at sunrise?

#3 What am I missing? Am I naive to think that what we are doing is enough to reverse a disease that seems even less-reversible compared to cancer in some cases? There seems to be so much more in the blogs these days about different protocols and specific treatments. I've been sticking to the '6 best healers' but I'm losing confidence and wondering if there's more.

The recent blogs have me second-guessing exogenous insulin, maybe throwing in a little LSD, stick with sunrise and grounding for sure, yet seek high altitude although we were told to be in the tropics grounding at the beach. The boys spearfish, but certainly don't 'deep dive'... how much can 2 teenage boys do?! And now the poles are flipping. Are we in a simulation? It's the matrix, isn't it?! J/K... I'm trying to stay positive and even find some light-heartedness in this but I gotta say, I'm feeling a little blown around in the wind.

Perhaps all of these thoughts are coming from a place of plateau-ing... the boys are somewhat at a stall at the moment.

Dr. Kruse, please tell me if this is truly reversible for them, or if I've just been living in la-la land hoping I (and nature, and God) can cure them? Just being real here; hopefully this is a place I can do that.

### Jaime C — 2026-04-20T11:28:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-371563>

Doing the best I can to keep up with these new blogs plus do all the things with the boys. My mind is blown. I'm just letting it all sink in as I read and re-read all of those words! I am able to absorb a lot of what Dr. Kruse is saying, but not quite ready to speak the lingo fluently... kinda like when learning a new language.

Just wanted to post here to mention a few positives I've seen in the 13 year old, to give thanks, and to rattle off some thoughts/questions...

I've only bought UNscented candles, and the kids know it - until there was a mega-sale on a batch that was barely-scented, so I grabbed them about a month ago. I've been burning them when needed, and Tucker has never mentioned a scent (it's hardly noticeable). He has been telling me for a year or two that he is 'nose blind' (his way of saying he can't smell most things!). Last night, he was near one of those barely-scented candles and he asked, "is this candle scented? no way you bought a scented candle?!...and I can smell it!?" Might be a stretch, but I'm encouraged that this is a positive effect of what we are doing. Tucker's ability to smell, along with the mention of the Olfactory system in the new blogs, caught my attention.

Another positive for him specifically - he voluntarily told me that while he has been more comfortable doing his own hobbies solo for a while, he feels like now he's ready to start making new friends. That conversation was not prompted, and I've never mentioned the solo-type personality attribute to him even though I've noticed that about him. He's always been pretty brilliant - ahead of his age for sure, but not super social. It reminded me of the typical 'loner' tendency described in a previous post.

Last thing about him... he is so happy; smiling and laughing and carefree. It's like the tension he generally carried is melting away in the sunshine.

I also wanted to post to humbly say Thank You to Dr. Kruse. Ultimately this is a battle for my boys' independence from Big Harma and insulin, and I am enjoying seeing the broader improvements in them along the way. This week, I feel I have been called 'defender of children'. This is not just for my own boys, but hopefully for others who also want to give the whole Rockefeller club the middle finger.

Things I'll be thinking about as I go to bed tonight...

Where did that Dr. G. Moody book go? Still looking for the Back Door that I'm missing. It's interesting that he used 'diet, movement, and environment'. I'd love to know what more we could be doing for those items. Maybe CT and probably specific exercises. I have noticed CT mentioned plenty, and I'm pretty sure the boys will battle me on cold plunges if I ask them to do that. Wondering if CT is crucial for them, and what that routine would look like.

The Godwit - reminds me of when the we did the PKD (Paleolithic-ketogenic diet) and they were completely insulin-free for 13 months. If I'm connecting the dots, it was because they were able to make the UPEs and run on their internal light created by being in therapeutic ketosis (1.5+) constantly. Eating mostly fat, meat from ruminants, and liver, gets old though. I know that's not the message for us, as food is not the answer. But based on the Godwit example, I can't help but think it's somewhat related. I'll be re-reading it again to try to figure out how my boys can be Godwits...magnetic vortex looks like the answer, and I'm not sure what more we could be doing to support that. We are already on black sand, volcanic rock, and/or in the ocean daily. Just takes time I'm sure.

I noticed the quick mention of how "We should be de-fragging them all before using insulin", and it was fun to chase the de-fragging into the vagus nerve blog!

Something else mentioned in that same post though... "The Lesson for Diabetics: The Godwit proves that you can have massive "Hyperglycemia" and "Insulin Resistance" and still be the most high-performance athlete on Earth, if you have the "Melanin Shielding" to manage the photo-electric load." Does that mean the boys could potentially not use insulin, run high blood sugars, but avoid DKA by being super tan and being pro surfers?

When being at the beach "GROUNDED" was mentioned earlier in this thread for the boys, and I reiterated that they are always barefoot/can't find their slippahs most of the time. After reading about the vagus nerve, I'm getting that GROUNDED perhaps means more than just barefoot on the black sand. We've been humming, turning our heads left to right, jumping jacks, pressing our tongues to the roofs of our mouths... maybe that helps. I feel like I'm trying to decode layers of riddles here!

Very thankful to Dr. Kruse for the information available! Sometimes I wish I could have a cute little recipe card that just tells me what to do - I would follow it! For now, I'll chase the leads and keep studying.

### Jaime C — 2026-04-21T09:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/type-1-diabetes-x2.31631/post-371680>

Well that's encouraging and I'm actually not sure if they have the capacity to get much more tan than they already are; they are getting pretty dark!

So, if high blood sugars are not the enemy, and we don't have to worry about DKA because of the melanin... time to decrease the insulin?

Because we've seen a few scary lows in the 40s over the last few weeks, I've been erring on the side of letting them be around 200 before correcting for now. At their current insulin dosages, that is where their BG levels hover. I have noticed though, around 225-275, they tend to feel sick and 'low' and hungry, so that's tricky to navigate.

The only metric I have to go off of is their 24-hr long-lasting dosage. They very rarely use any fast-acting insulin since we eat low-carb/keto.

Their dosages recently:

Jaxon (age 15): 32 units in CO / 15 units in HI
Tucker (age 13): 35 units in CO / 26 units in HI

Neither of them have ever had a pump and they don't use CGMs. They finger poke to check BG 3-4 x daily and I'm always filled with anticipation of potentially lower levels.

Will they see a true reversal and be able to go back down to non-diabetic blood sugar levels, or will this be more of a "use IR as your superpower and as long as you're tan, you won't go into DKA?".

I had been thinking about whether “free will” exists before finding Jack, but with all the emphasis on light and environment, isn’t it obvious that “free will” is just an illusion?

Nature never makes mistakes - every step of evolution and adaptation of life is a response (even if stochastic in nature) to the environment. Environment is shaped by this life and on it goes.

Every thought you have traces back to some sequence of electrons, photons, etc.

Thinking you thought a thought is just a human post-hoc rationalization of quantum phenomena.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-09-13T04:55:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/free-will.31632/post-359444>

Can you have free will if you cannot control your hormone panels?

Check out my latest article: ROCKEFELLER MEDICINE WAS THE SEED THAT BECAME MKULTRA [https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/rockefeller-medicine-seed-became-mkultra-jack-kruse-kmpff](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/rockefeller-medicine-seed-became-mkultra-jack-kruse-kmpff)

Heme (in CYP enzymes like CYP11A1, CYP19A1) is chiral and absorbs red wavelengths preferentially, enabling spin-selective electron transport via CISS. This regulates protein folding, enzyme activity, and hormonal output:

Spin-Selective Interactions: Biophotons from sources like NADH, cytochrome c, porphyrins (heme), ROS, or melanin carry spin that matches chiral heme structures, triggering excitation (right-handed) or absorption (left-handed). Red light's low-energy spin aligns with heme's Q-band absorption, promoting coherent electron flow in the ETC and CYP reactions without oxidative damage. Higher-frequency colors (e.g., blue) induce chaotic UPEs, altering iron oxidation states, increasing RBC turnover, and disrupting CYP heme, leading to pregnenolone steal and infertility.

Quantum Coherence and Morphogenesis: As per Turing's 1952 paper on morphogenesis, morphogens (now seen as UPE-driven in my light model) sculpt tissues via reaction-diffusion instabilities. Red light's preserved helicity maintains quantum coherence in water CDs and microtubules, optimizing AMO for protein bending (tertiary/quaternary structures) in CYP enzymes. This explains why red acts as a natural aromatase inhibitor (CYP19A1), balancing estrogens without the high-energy spin chaos of blue light, which dehydrates melanin and lowers éR = energy resistance.

Low éR = Efficiency and Flow: In healthy states, like during deep sleep or a good workout recovery, éR is dialed down. Energy transforms smoothly—think laminar flow in a river, minimal turbulence. Your cells rebuild, adapt, and thrive.

Physics analogy: It's like a superconductor with near-zero resistance.

Blue light causes more noise and less signal, and this is how you alter gender with light, allowing current (or bioenergy) to zip through without loss. Blue light causes more noise, less signal, and this is how you alter gender with light.

Evolutionary Relevance: During the GOE, heme/melanin evolved to handle UV/oxygen via spin-selective photon interactions. Red light's spin favors repair (exciton release), while shorter wavelengths cause absorption/damage, linking to spectrum disorders in hormones.

**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Pete Collins — 2025-09-12T02:37:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-is-the-best-place-to-find-recent-accurate-research-on-harms-of-technology.31624/post-359400>

Right here & Jacks many many blogs

### Mat.I — 2025-09-12T03:47:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-is-the-best-place-to-find-recent-accurate-research-on-harms-of-technology.31624/post-359404>

I have posted others some where on here before. Some links below -

### [EMF-Portal | Home](https://www.emf-portal.org/en)

 www.emf-portal.org

### [Electromagnetic Radiation Safety](https://www.saferemr.com/)

Health effects of exposure to wireless devices, including cordless & cell phones, cell towers, Wi-Fi & Smart Meters, & harm reduction strategies.

 www.saferemr.com

### [BioInitiative Report: A Rationale for a Biologically-based Public Exposure Standard for Electromagnetic Fields (ELF and RF)](https://bioinitiative.org/)

An international working group of scientists, researchers and public health policy professionals (The BioInitiative Working Group) has released its report on electromagnetic fields (EMF) and health. They document serious scientific concerns about current limits regulating how much EMF is...

bioinitiative.org

Enjoy..

### Anthony Smith — 2025-11-05T04:49:04+0200

# Thread: Encountering personal obstacles to healing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### K-in-Oz — 2025-09-12T12:42:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359410>

Life is often a series of difficult choices. Some of them feel impossible. That's where I am right now. I live in the southern part of Australia. And my health deteriorates beginning in March every year, and gets worse and worse until Summer finally arrives with relief. But just when I'm finally starting to feel better, Autumn arrives too soon. I'm also a student, so getting way too much computer time (no good, I know).

My sort-of-other-half also has lots of health issues. I had hopes of convincing him to move up further north, to get more of that good sunshine all year. But this year, our relationship crashed and burned. Separated since May, but living together still with our 4-year-old son. Now arises the problem. My health seems to deteriorate even further every year, and lately I'm starting to feel like really bad stuff is imminent. My body is telling me it's life or death if I don't act soon. But my son's dad doesn't want to move, so we are in a sticky position with regard to custody.

I am facing the possibility of leaving without my son. Not something I would ever have considered under any other circumstances. Not something most parents want to be in the position to consider. But I can't function properly physically or mentally. And I don't think I will have a strong case in court, although I will certainly try (can't prove to them I'm as unwell as I know I am, can't prove to mainstream entities that this is actually the answer, no support up north, no particular qualifications for a good job). I feel I'm at the most horrifying crossroads I've yet faced in my 32 years.

A lot of personal words to say that if anyone else has faced any extremely difficult personal situations on their health journey and has words of advice, I'd love to hear them.

### Mat.I — 2025-09-12T14:10:56+0300

Sorry to hear this. I have experienced very testing and challenging times in my life also, that I.would not change as wounds create your wisdom.. It can feel like you will.never make it out, but it is important to embrace the suck and keep your head up and keep moving towards the goal. Stay positive. If you cannot get all the way up North is there some middle ground you can both agree on?? If you cannot get on your own feet at the moment work towards this so you have a fail safe to fall back on if necessary.. Do what you can to.improve your current environment if the move will not happen for a while.. Have you knocked off the basics such.as mitigation of nn-emf? Important to focus on.the now and do what you can knowing we are in spring heading to summer shortly so you do have that working in your favour.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-09-13T19:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359461>

The first paragraph tells you what you should do.......will you listen to the whispers of Nature in your ear or keep doing what you have done in the past?

### K-in-Oz — 2025-09-15T03:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359518>

> [Mat.I said:](/goto/post?id=359412)
>
> Sorry to hear this. I have experienced very testing and challenging times in my life also, that I.would not change as wounds create your wisdom.. It can feel like you will.never make it out, but it is important to embrace the suck and keep your head up and keep moving towards the goal. Stay positive. If you cannot get all the way up North is there some middle ground you can both agree on?? If you cannot get on your own feet at the moment work towards this so you have a fail safe to fall back on if necessary.. Do what you can to.improve your current environment if the move will not happen for a while.. Have you knocked off the basics such.as mitigation of nn-emf? Important to focus on.the now and do what you can knowing we are in spring heading to summer shortly so you do have that working in your favour.

Thanks for your reply, Mat, I appreciate it. The weather is certainly starting to get nicer now that spring is here. We actually have a holiday booked to Cairns next month, so that will be very helpful, and I'm hoping it will be persuasive to my son's dad. We actually booked it because my son is in daycare and has suffered a lot of illness to the point that he's had enlarged tonsils and adenoids for a while now, which is affecting his ability to breathe at night when he sleeps. We visited an ENT who took one look and, of course, told us he would chop them straight out. Which we absolutely have no intention of doing. Hence, booking the holiday instead to get some sun healing. I just need to convince my son's dad that we need something more long term, not just a holiday. He is averse to the move because his business is set up here and he is not fully on board with all of this stuff. I am still working to convince him.

As for the nn-emf situation here, it's certainly not ideal. We have wifi which I turn off at night and when it is not needed. We are renters, though, so I'm not sure what else we could do really. I also lost my footing with everything a bit when winter came and my mental health nose dived with winter and I was really struggling with uni work and then I started falling back on bad habits. Everyone around me also just thinks I'm a looney tune with all this and I let it slip away from me. I think it has just affirmed for me how much harder everything gets when I don't have the right inputs. In summer, it was not difficult to do the right thing. I am amazed at how I went from holding this information in my head and my hands and knowing what I needed to do to just slipping into unconciousness again. But then it got so bad that something inside me started screaming and that has helped to wake me up again. But I think it has made everything crystal clear now. I've seen the pattern and I know I need to avoid getting complacent when summer comes and I'm feeling better. It's certainly been a learning curve, too. I will freely admit to having felt like the victim of circumstances in the past. And in a way, I won't say that I don't think thats true (to an extent, given that for the vast majority of us, we at first do not know that any of this stuff we grew up with is so deleterious). But I didn't have the information before. I have it now, and I've seen the pattern, and the reality is, no one is going to fix this for me so I am hardening my resolve. I've had mental health issues since I was a kid, so I've been in the low-agency pattern of being treated like I'm not sound of mind, and just accepting that. But now I see the truth. That yes, I can be unwell and sometimes I am not sound of mind. But there are reasons for that. I see that now. I am not just inherently unwell or insane. I know what needs to happen now and it's no longer a maybe or a "nice to have" in my mind. It is not enough to simply stay here and do what I can. Although I will of course do what I can in the meantime.

### Pete Collins — 2025-09-15T03:20:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359519>

You are precisely where you need to be right now, reading what you say, you are cracking open, breaking apart the old, it hurts you to feel it, expand your thinking, embrace the pain and chaos, you will be guided if your will is strong, what you need in life awaits you, are you willing to jump off the wall & dive into the light?

### K-in-Oz — 2025-09-15T03:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359520>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=359461)
>
> The forst paragraph tells you what you should do.......will you listen to the whispers of Nature in your ear or keep doing what you have done in the past?

Thanks for your reply Dr Kruse. And you are right. The pattern has been clearly laid out for me. It is obvious to me what needs to happen. And now I know that I need to make it happen regardless of anything else. Because if I don't. And if I wake up in 5 or 10 years, or even less, with something far more serious on my hands, I will have no one else to blame but myself. Because the writing is on the wall in big, bold, red letters.

### K-in-Oz — 2025-09-15T03:26:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359521>

> [Pete Collins said:](/goto/post?id=359519)

Thanks for your response Pete. I appreciate it. It is nice to hear from people who understand. I can't pretend that I am not human and that it is not affirming to have another human acknowledge these things. And you are right that it hurts to feel it. It absolutely does. Shedding the old thinking of "oh but I can't do that, people can't just do anything they have to do, and how could I do that because of x, y, z" and realising that actually yes, I can do it. It is a choice. It may be an extremely difficult choice, but it is still a choice. And I can't choose a slow death anymore. I choose life.

I couldn't bare to leave my children behind, I think that would break me faster than anything else. Can you not work more closely with the natural environment around you? I would love to move to FNQLD and it is on the cards for the future.

### K-in-Oz — 2025-09-15T07:49:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359525>

> [MayaB said:](/goto/post?id=359523)
>
> I couldn't bare to leave my children behind, I think that would break me faster than anything else. Can you not work more closely with the natural environment around you? I would love to move to FNQLD and it is on the cards for the future.

I don't think it's enough for me, unfortunately. I would not consider leaving my son behind if I felt like there was another reasonable solution. My physical and mental health tank seasonally every year, and I seem to be having additional problems every year. The mental health issues have been there since I was around 11, and I am 32 now. In my 20s came the IBS-type issues, which eventually got to the point that I have allergic-like reactions to many foods, but I have been eating a rather strict/simple diet for some time now. And during the worst of it, I don't think a year has passed in my life when I don't go through periods of suicidal thoughts and just the general feeling of my mind being fragmented. And it was last year that the more strange symptoms started, face rashes where my face goes red and feels like it's burning, along with similar sensations in my hands/feet/knees, repeated bouts of vertigo last year (none this year, fortunately, although I had some days recently where I had begun to feel dizzy but it went away). The warmer weather is coming now, and today is lovely, and man, am I _feeling_ that in my bones haha. But I don't want to keep doing this year after year, and I would be very surprised if I managed to totally avoid similar outcomes next year, and based on the way things seem to be progressing, I think I need better long-term healing to avoid worse outcomes in future. Leaving my son behind is not something I see as a long-term thing, and of course, if I can avoid that altogether, that's what I want to do. I want us all to move, and I feel like that's what my son needs as well, considering what he has inherited from me and his dad (who has had a liver transplant due to autoimmune illness and has two other autoimmune conditions). My hope is that I can convince my son's dad that the move is a good idea, but if I can't do it through words, my second hope is that I can convince him through taking the plunge myself and showing him the difference it makes.

### Mat.I — 2025-09-16T05:37:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/encountering-personal-obstacles-to-healing.31635/post-359558>

> [K-in-Oz said:](/goto/post?id=359518)
>
> We actually booked it because my son is in daycare and has suffered a lot of illness to the point that he's had enlarged tonsils and adenoids for a while now, which is affecting his ability to breathe at night when he sleeps.

Try to manage this with sleep positions (typically on side and pillow slightly elevated). But fixing the nn-emf & getting good light typically resolves this, I have witnessed this in two other children around this age with dramatic improvements within 6 months of changes be made.

Day care's are very nasty for health + you require sunscreen exemptions. My advice is get him out of that environment as soon as you can (obviously your health permitting)..

> [K-in-Oz said:](/goto/post?id=359518)
>
> As for the nn-emf situation here, it's certainly not ideal. We have wifi which I turn off at night and when it is not needed. We are renters, though, so I'm not sure what else we could do really. But I didn't have the information before. I have it now, and I've seen the pattern, and the reality is, no one is going to fix this for me so I am hardening my resolve. I've had mental health issues since I was a kid, so I've been in the low-agency pattern of being treated like I'm not sound of mind, and just accepting that. But now I see the truth. That yes, I can be unwell and sometimes I am not sound of mind. But there are reasons for that. I see that now. I am not just inherently unwell or insane.
