# Thread: Does all electricity use generate radiation?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-all-electricity-use-generate-radiation.31934/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-25T04:13:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-all-electricity-use-generate-radiation.31934/post-363120>

Does all electricity use generate radiation?

Yes, it is true. All electricity use from our power grid generates radiation, specifically extremely low-frequency (ELF) electromagnetic fields (EMF) from the movement of electric current in power lines, wiring, and devices. This radiation, which includes both electric and magnetic fields, is a form of non-ionizing radiation and is present whenever current flows, from household appliances to high-tension power lines.

Sources of radiation

-   [Power lines](https://www.google.com/search?q=Power+lines&client=safari&sca_esv=9d19827db9edfb9f&sxsrf=AE3TifO-9nUQfBfJ_Rj9h5GaUklwL9xm0A%3A1764036398743&source=hp&ei=Lg8laZv8KqyPwbkPyOGo4Qc&iflsig=AOw8s4IAAAAAaSUdPiM2fip6IizMK7cluqrj_zLpOlbq&ved=2ahUKEwiAsfHim4yRAxXBUjABHarmGJ0QgK4QegQIAxAB&uact=5&oq=does+all+electricity+use+generate+radiation%3F&gs_lp=Egdnd3Mtd2l6Iixkb2VzIGFsbCBlbGVjdHJpY2l0eSB1c2UgZ2VuZXJhdGUgcmFkaWF0aW9uPzIIEAAYFhgKGB4yCxAAGIAEGIYDGIoFMgsQABiABBiGAxiKBTILEAAYgAQYhgMYigUyCxAAGIAEGIYDGIoFMgsQABiABBiGAxiKBTIFEAAY7wUyBRAAGO8FMgUQABjvBUiSqQFQAFi3ogFwAngAkAEAmAHQAaAB-&sclient=gws-wiz&mstk=AUtExfB_rBk8rtQkQeitqS0HvZc5ggGx5IS6_enfAM0T6IbwhHf-Wu5MB5KP6PPiDV7ObQjnrgrlPiOJEN7fplRYJ9Zqu1Q96kR4mbYtXNWQMO3znsL6W8HpNWsCTwev8tGa_Ho6Wvb6mafTsFtIbypisVaV3SRq7IsMCSJ3FtkKruHT-qU&csui=3) and wiring: Any electrical conductor carrying current, including overhead power lines and the wiring in your home, produces EMF.
-   Household appliances: Almost all electrical devices generate ELF-EMF when turned on, including refrigerators, vacuum cleaners, hair dryers, and computers.
-   Other electronic devices: Other products, such as televisions and some smart devices, also emit radiation.
-   Electrical current: The radiation is generated by the acceleration and deceleration of electrons as a current flows through a circuit.

Is all this radiation generated polarized?

Yes, virtually all electricity use generates electromagnetic radiation (EMR).
Moreover, this man-made radiation is polarized extremely low frequency (ELF) radiation.

Polarization of the Radiation

-   Man-made EMR is Polarized: Unlike most natural EMR (such as sunlight), which is unpolarized (oscillates in random orientations), the EMR from human-made electrical sources is polarized. Melanin is the only protein capable of handling polarized light.
-   Fixed Orientation: Man-made EMR is produced by electrons oscillating in a specific, forced direction along wires or within circuits. This gives the electric and magnetic fields a defined, consistent plane of oscillation (linear, circular, or elliptical polarization depending on the source geometry, e.g., 3-phase power lines produce circularly/elliptically polarized fields).
-   Biological Implications: Some scientists theorize that the polarized nature of man-made EMFs, rather than their intensity, might be a key factor in their potential biological effects, as this coordinated oscillation can force charged molecules within biological tissues to move in a specific, unnatural way. Health agencies such as the [World Health Organization (WHO)](https://www.who.int/news-room/questions-and-answers/item/radiation-electromagnetic-fields) and the [National Cancer Institute (NCI)](https://www.cancer.gov/about-cancer/causes-prevention/risk/radiation/electromagnetic-fields-fact-sheet)continue to study potential health effects, classifying ELF fields as "possibly carcinogenic to humans (group 2B)" based on some epidemiological studies suggesting an increased risk of childhood leukemia.

### knees4bukele — 2025-11-30T18:58:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-all-electricity-use-generate-radiation.31934/post-363398>

You mentioned to put all 1000 yards far away from the place you live. I'm curious about the solar panels by themselves why are them so bad? I'm pretty aware about the inverters but with the Gauss meter we have [GQ EMF 390](https://lessemf.com/product/gq-emf-390-combination-meter/) and I have not noticed anything there I hope is not shitty meter and I have to purchase new one to detect that

The main problem with solar is the inversion from DC to AC. This creates high frequency transients (dirty electricity) that ride on the base AC frequency. Most electronics also invert from AC back to DC using switch mode power supplies, which create additional high frequency transients.

Keeping it DC only gets rid of the inverter problem and is much more efficient, as you no longer need to do two inversions to power your electronics.

I like doing small 12V DC solar systems as you can find a lot of electronics/appliances that run on 12V DC.
For example: My computer, modem, and wired ethernet router can all run directly off of a 12V DC battery with no step-up or step-down conversions needed. I also have a small fridge that runs on 12V DC as well. There's lots of options out there.

12V DC wiring is super simple to learn as well for the DIY type.

# Thread: GATE Program during the 90s
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/>
**Subforum:** Dive Deeper with Dr. Kruse’s Exclusive Webinar!
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KFDags — 2025-11-25T21:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-363178>

It’s been a few months now since I first saw some videos out there (first on X) regarding the 90s Gifted and Talented Education (GATE) program and its alleged CIA involvement with mind control, remote viewing/psychic abilities, and even recruiting future agents. Some have compared it to the Stargate Project, which was a real operation. Not sure if anyone is familiar with what I’m rambling on about; I know another member brought this up in the forum months ago. He was from the same area as me, Rhode Island, although this program took place all over the US and a few in some other countries. Well, all I can say is I’m utterly weirded out. Why are all these former GATE students appearing to be coming out now, at the same time? I believe in coincidences less and less, the older I get.

My first instinct is to distrust whatever it is being blasted out there on the internet, but I can’t ignore the staggering number of similarities between these so-called GATE participants and me. Like many others in this program back in the 90s, they took me and some other students out of class (I was probably ~11 years old.) We were given the hearing tests with the giant headphones in a secluded room. If you heard a sound, you’d raise a hand or point to the corresponding ear. (i.e., raise your left hand if you hear a sound in the left ear, and do the opposite for the right side.) I also had some test done at the school that they claimed was to check for scoliosis. Up until recently I had assumed that everyone had these tests done in school, right? I do recall the pink liquid in cups (was told it’s a fluoride rinse,) the pink chewable tablets (was told it’s to highlight dental plaque) but I can’t remember if I actually used any of them.

However it started, I found myself in the Crusaders (or Crusades) Club. My mom remembers me being in this club but never knowing why. Once a week, I was pulled out of class to go be in this window-less room inside the school, along with some others. There were college-age students there helping us, mostly girls; they always dressed in light-colored but neutral clothing, oversized sweatshirts, matching sweatpants. One girl looked to me like Alanis Morissette; when I told her that, she got kind of offended. That’s the timeframe for when this was happening: I had my Jagged Little Pill cassette tape and cassette player with me for the bus ride so 1996 maybe.

Each new time we arrived to this club, there was a table with one or two piles of printed-out papers with puzzles or some type of word problem to solve. The papers were always just there to take, but we never had a teacher or any instruction. At first, no one made me do the work so I went with it, of course. (Duh.) But I began to get the sense that there was a reason that the work was there. Again, no one ever said we had to do this work. I started participating eventually. We also never got graded or any results.

Other than that, we just did fun activities to pass the time: watched movies, hung out, ate junk food, etc. For some reason, the only movie I remember we watched was The Ugly Dachshund, from 1966. The Crusades Club had no instructor or instructions and there was never any direction; it was unlike any other class I’ve had before or after.

I’ll post more to this strangeness when I get some more time. Just wanted to put it down here since maybe someone else has some substance to offer or maybe their own experience. Anytime I bring these subjects up with my family, all I get in reply is, “Oh,” or “I don’t remember.”

### KFDags — 2025-12-05T20:54:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-363615>

Over Thanksgiving, I asked my parents (they’re divorced) if they recalled the Crusades program I was in. My dad actually did: he recalled my mom being very excited over me being offered a spot in some special “enrichment program” for high IQ/high abilty children. He described the program as some type of additional education or special educational opportunities, separate from my regular classes.

When speaking with my mom, she remembered almost nothing (naturally.) She did say that my uncle suggested that I transfer to some school, in the City of Providence, that had a special giftedness program. That obviously never happened.

As an aside, this tiny detail (that I was, in fact, a gifted/high-ability child) is something that was kept from me until now, at nearly 40 years old. My parents’ obliviousness has been a lifelong frustration for me. I do love them, but I’m now having an identity crisis and re-thinking my entire existence on this random December afternoon.

### KFDags — 2025-12-05T23:29:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-363622>

Yes! They sound creepy as hell! Does the futuristic beeping bother anyone else on a visceral level or???

### doctorwu85 — 2025-12-08T03:09:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-363680>

That was me: [https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads...grams-an-offshoot-of-mkultra-and-darpa.30998/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-90s-public-school-gifted-programs-an-offshoot-of-mkultra-and-darpa.30998/)

### HLG2025 — 2025-12-17T12:48:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-363987>

I was also in the “gifted program” from (asked/tested to be in it in 1st grade) 2nd grade onwards. This was in Miami, Florida. For me, it was the entirety of my class was a gifted class so i wasnt pulled out of normal class for a certain period of time to go into a special program, it was just i was totally in a gifted class for the base of my schooling, In middle school and high school electives were mostly not gifted so put with the normies and also AP classes were mixed with normies as well (so like kids who were in “honors” classes would go into AP classes sometimes). I graduated 44 out of 844 in my class with minimal effort. I dont recall anything strange or weird although i kind of wish i did because that woukd be a little more exciting…but i do agree that suddenly im hearing about this (im also almost 40) when i hadn’t seen or heard anything about it my entire life!! (i also relate to my parents obliviousness being a lifelong frustration, as my mom also remembers nothing)

### KFDags — 2026-02-09T21:49:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-366606>

For those unaware, this is the list of very common characteristics we former GATE students apparently share. No one checks off everything from this list, but most can say yes to the majority. Can’t speak for others here, but I have exactly zero in common with most other humans, mostly by choice. So finding all these former GATE students with the exact same qualities and experiences is disturbing.

### KFDags — 2026-02-10T23:07:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-366654>

At the same time the GATE classes started, mid-90s, my elementary school got its first DARE officer, Officer Currier. I thought it was great: he interrupted our mundane class, once a week, for an hour or so; we listened to 90s grunge, which I was all about; he talked about having positive self-esteem, avoiding drugs, not joining gangs, the whole thing. I tuned out all the fluff; no need to repeat the obvious.

Before he ended each DARE class, he’d do animal trivia. No one else in class knew jack shit about wildlife, so I won all the prizes, nearly every class. Also provided my guidance when he had trouble pronouncing the hard words. He stared confused when I explained that I wanted to be a herpetologist someday. He also learned the definition of herpetologist.

Officer Currier (now a school resource officer, no longer doing DARE) then showed up to my middle school, once I transferred there. I didn’t see him as much or as predictably as before, but he was always within reach, it felt. I thought it was comforting? He’d never fail to greet me by stating my full first and last name out loud.

Once, Officer Currier took me to his office; he showed me that a classmate had called me some nasty names, then posted it online. Just dumb teenager BS, back even before MySpace existed; nothing even remotely serious. Still never figured out how he found that post. Don’t know what happened to my classmate, but Officer Currier was pissed; way more than I was.

Then, 1999/2000, he followed me to my high school. I mentioned in my optimal journal how I used to skip school, almost constantly, to sleep. Well, had it not been for Officer Currier, I never would have gotten away with it! For 3 straight years. He knew my car and license plate so the rest was history.

At 17, I began my first serious relationship with my boyfriend. He was staying with mutual friends of ours, during this time, around 2003. I had never been to this friend’s house before. Guess who lived directly across the street from my boyfriend? None other than Officer Currier!

I never saw or heard from him again, though. By this time, I was working at a local pet store. That is exactly when my creepy stalker customer first appeared. His occupation? Law enforcement. Stay tuned!

### KFDags — 2026-02-11T19:35:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-366703>

Here’s the man himself, Officer Currier. The second picture is from the high school yearbook. Hey, if I’m on a list, may as well have fun with it, right?

### JenniferM — 2026-02-11T21:43:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-366717>

Wow! This is such a creepy story. Right up there with serial killers/stalkers.

### KFDags — 2026-02-13T00:11:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-366782>

> [JenniferM said:](/goto/post?id=366717)
>
> Wow! This is such a creepy story. Right up there with serial killers/stalkers.

The thing is, he was always very kind & respectful to me. I never imagined something nefarious. And before I go any further, I will acknowledge that the bitter irony is not lost on me: my husband is a police officer. I am so deeply entrenched in whatever this thing is, it’s not even funny. How many lifelong coincidences equal a true living nightmare?

Anyway, the pet store was different. This super tall, muscular guy named Chris used to visit often, just to hang out; this started before I was hired. He was a cop, maybe early to mid-30s, married, and had twin baby girls. Total scumbag. Was previously lurking around my two female coworkers (including the owner’s sister), but then I became the favorite. He was extremely handsy, to put it lightly. I repeatedly complained to my boss to not leave me alone with him, but he did nothing. Never bothered to protect his own sister from him either, so no surprise there.

I quit and began working at a different pet store in another town. Figured I was all set. Then Chris started calling my cell phone and showing up at my new job. I’m still 17, by the way. I never gave him my info and couldn’t fathom how this happened. No need to ask because he explained that he took some belongings off my former boss’ desk, which contained my information.

I can’t remember what my parents said or did about this, if anything at all; and my boyfriend was too busy constantly celebrating being 21 years old, back then. So he kept showing up for a while, without any regard for the other customers there; he’d be there lurking. I didn’t know what to do. But abruptly, he disappeared one day. Stopped showing up, never called again, and that was it.

### flavin0 — 2026-02-16T05:17:23+0200

I got put in a gifted youth program at Stanford for a summer, though it wasn't called GATE. I just took a college class with some interesting people, living on campus for the summer. Maybe it was a cover.

I believe one of my favorite YouTubers named Jay Dyer got put in the GATE program as well. His work is great.

### Woodstock — 2026-02-24T06:04:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gate-program-during-the-90s.31937/post-367549>

> [KFDags said:](/goto/post?id=363178)
>
> It’s been a few months now since I first saw some videos out there (first on X) regarding the 90s Gifted and Talented Education (GATE) program and its alleged CIA involvement with mind control, remote viewing/psychic abilities, and even recruiting future agents. Some have compared it to the Stargate Project, which was a real operation. Not sure if anyone is familiar with what I’m rambling on about; I know another member brought this up in the forum months ago. He was from the same area as me, Rhode Island, although this program took place all over the US and a few in some other countries. Well, all I can say is I’m utterly weirded out. Why are all these former GATE students appearing to be coming out now, at the same time? I believe in coincidences less and less, the older I get.
>
> My first instinct is to distrust whatever it is being blasted out there on the internet, but I can’t ignore the staggering number of similarities between these so-called GATE participants and me. Like many others in this program back in the 90s, they took me and some other students out of class (I was probably ~11 years old.) We were given the hearing tests with the giant headphones in a secluded room. If you heard a sound, you’d raise a hand or point to the corresponding ear. (i.e., raise your left hand if you hear a sound in the left ear, and do the opposite for the right side.) I also had some test done at the school that they claimed was to check for scoliosis. Up until recently I had assumed that everyone had these tests done in school, right? I do recall the pink liquid in cups (was told it’s a fluoride rinse,) the pink chewable tablets (was told it’s to highlight dental plaque) but I can’t remember if I actually used any of them.
>
> However it started, I found myself in the Crusaders (or Crusades) Club. My mom remembers me being in this club but never knowing why. Once a week, I was pulled out of class to go be in this window-less room inside the school, along with some others. There were college-age students there helping us, mostly girls; they always dressed in light-colored but neutral clothing, oversized sweatshirts, matching sweatpants. One girl looked to me like Alanis Morissette; when I told her that, she got kind of offended. That’s the timeframe for when this was happening: I had my Jagged Little Pill cassette tape and cassette player with me for the bus ride so 1996 maybe.
>
> Each new time we arrived to this club, there was a table with one or two piles of printed-out papers with puzzles or some type of word problem to solve. The papers were always just there to take, but we never had a teacher or any instruction. At first, no one made me do the work so I went with it, of course. (Duh.) But I began to get the sense that there was a reason that the work was there. Again, no one ever said we had to do this work. I started participating eventually. We also never got graded or any results.
>
> Other than that, we just did fun activities to pass the time: watched movies, hung out, ate junk food, etc. For some reason, the only movie I remember we watched was The Ugly Dachshund, from 1966. The Crusades Club had no instructor or instructions and there was never any direction; it was unlike any other class I’ve had before or after.
>
> I’ll post more to this strangeness when I get some more time. Just wanted to put it down here since maybe someone else has some substance to offer or maybe their own experience. Anytime I bring these subjects up with my family, all I get in reply is, “Oh,” or “I don’t remember.”

All the tests you mentioned in the beginning were done to all or most children. I remember them from the 70s.

I was never in the “gifted” program. My mom told us that me and all my siblings could have been in it because of very high IQs but my parents would not allow it. My Dad knew there was something off about it.

I don’t know much else except it seemed like a cool thing.

I was always wondering why my 3 older siblings all got to be on the “safety patrol” and I didn’t. My IQ was in the same range, high 130s, but I was not chosen for safety patrol. I got good grades.

Now I realize that’s it’s because I’m on the spectrum. They didn’t call it that then. And girls were ignored because the symptoms present differently. But I took a bunch of online tests because of the overlap with autism and Lyme disease. Now I realize it’s mitochondrial damage at the root cause.

Interesting to find out that you weren t being taught anything special.

Glad my Dad was a genius and knew.

### BruceKing — 2025-11-27T14:48:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-363254>

J. Christopher Giancarlo, former Chairman of the CFTC -
In a 2019 interview about the 2017 bubble, Giancarlo described how U.S. regulators viewed Bitcoin futures as a way to deflate the mania:
He told CoinDesk that the CFTC, Treasury, SEC, and NEC (Gary Cohn) “believed that the launch of bitcoin futures would have the impact of popping the bitcoin bubble. And it worked.” Institutional capital dominates. Large players can influence direction. Arbitrage forces spot markets to follow futures (whichever way they want to rig it). The U.S. establishes control over a decentralized asset.
They have done the same thing with silver for a very long time. The main difference is that you need silver to make stuff and it keeps getting "destroyed", BTC is only a store of value with a hard cap that keeps getting mined and not destroyed. I think this makes silver harder to manipulate in paper/synthetic markets because eventually you have to deliver a tangible asset that is getting costlier to recover from the earth. We are kind of seeing this right now in the silver market as December delivery month is one of the highest in history and the LBMA recently ran out of silver to deliver. Not financial advice and I don't have a crystal ball but hard assets that are needed by industry might be short term better. Still like BTC as a decentralized currency, though.

### BruceKing — 2025-11-28T22:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-363335>

They don’t need to confiscate Bitcoin.
They **already** control Bitcoin the same way they control every asset that threatens the existing power structure: wrap it in a cash-settled futures market, drown it in synthetic supply, and dictate price without ever touching the underlying.

It’s the same script they executed on silver for 40+ years —
**but they made one fatal mistake this time: silver is not Bitcoin.**

Bitcoin doesn’t build a single physical object on this planet.
No factory stops running if Bitcoin disappears.
That’s why BTC’s price can be puppeteered indefinitely with paper.

Silver has no such weakness.

Silver is the highest-conductivity metal known to man — an _industrial keystone_ of the modern world. Solar panels, EVs, medical tech, optics, 5G hardware, satellites, water purification, AI server farms — all of them require **physical silver atoms**. Not “unallocated accounts.” Not “warehouse receipts.” Not “credits.”
Actual. Physical. Metal.

And here’s where the nuclear core goes critical:

### The physical silver shortage is NOT hypothetical.​

It’s happening right now — in real time.\*\*

-   **COMEX registered silver has collapsed** from over 150 million oz to around 70 million.
-   **LBMA vaults have lost nearly half their silver** in just a few years.
-   Industrial demand is at **all-time highs** and accelerating.
-   Global inventories are being **drained simultaneously** by industry, sovereign mints, and private stackers.
-   Ore grades have plunged 87% in the last century.
-   70% of new silver is a byproduct — meaning **the supply cannot scale even if the price explodes**.

This is a _mathematical impossibility_ for the suppression system.

COMEX trades **hundreds of ounces of paper silver for every one ounce in the vaults** —
a **fractional-reserve metals system** that only functions as long as nobody asks for the bars.

But the industrial sector _is_ asking.
They need silver to build actual stuff.
And they can’t run factories on a COMEX contract.

### **This is the exact failure mode that blew up the London Gold Pool in 1968.**​

Too many claims, not enough metal. Sound familiar?

JP Morgan literally paid **the largest manipulation fines in U.S. history** for rigging precious metals — but even they can’t conjure atoms out of thin air. The paper system is now being forced to answer to geology, and geology doesn’t negotiate. Remember, Mother Nature is a ruthless bitch.

Bitcoin can be suppressed forever because derivatives can outnumber coins endlessly.

Silver can’t because you can’t cheat physics.

And here’s the part that should terrify the PTB:

### Once industry competes with investors for physical, the entire LBMA/COMEX complex goes supercritical.​

Not reprice — _fail_.

When one link breaks — a missed delivery, a failed rollover, a refinery declaring force majeure — the paper market goes the way of Lehman: slowly at first, then all at once.

Not financial advice, but…

Silver has a _minimum_ two-year industrial supercycle baked in —
and the blast wave has already begun.

### BruceKing — 2025-12-06T18:54:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-363639>

Have to backpedal a little on some of my previous views. After careful consideration that I might be wrong I need to clarify that there probably will not be any kind hyperinflation + complete banking collapse + global monetary reset. Realistically, this is highly unlikely. I think we will continue with the slow erosion of our purchasing power with accelerations and slowdowns. For hyperinflation we need to have: 1) A productivity collapse. Manufacturing is returning to the U.S. and the dollar is weakening incentivizing manufacturers to produce and sell products here and abroad (hyperinflation requires too much money chasing too few products) and export from the U.S. 2) Money supply expanding massively with velocity (got to have velocity to have hyperinflation). Boomers are retiring and spending less, fewer children from more recent generations who are already deep in debt, tends to slow spending. 3) The FED wants to survive so they are now more target liquidity oriented and more inflation sensitive (yes, I know lowering interest rates into inflation is not that inflation sensitive, but history shows they like to cycle this stuff with loose then tight monetary policy). 4) The government would never allow a massive banking collapse (voters would put politicians out on their ass when their retirement accounts are wiped out). Yes, the too big to fail banks will rip us off, again, privatize profits and socialize losses.
More later.

I latched onto the Rome analogy. In some ways Rome's slow decline punctuated by crisis is similar to the U.S. and in some ways it is not. We all know about currency debasement but many do not figure in the fact that the U.S. has vast energy reserves, tons of minerals, massive agriculture, gigantic forest, friendly neighbors (more or less), and ocean between us and hostile countries (greatest navy on earth), not to mention that under Trump we are re-industrializing our country which stabilizes currency and adds wages. Now, how much AI and robots are going to disenfranchise workers remains to be seen, but it should be balanced by more new manufacturing and increased productivity. There are forces pulling in both directions, so I can't really agree with Glen Beck's assessment, it just ends up being clickbait when you actually look at the details. Have to agree on the banking cartel getting what they want, but we kind of know what they want, to continue the game they are already playing. A major crisis really doesn't benefit anyone.

3) Control health (and kill people so you don't have to pay for them when they get old) We end up with a self-policing population that worships centralized medical authority...to the grave. Covid scare etc.
4) Blue light everywhere to destroy circadian rhythms with artificial light, overstimulation, poor sleep, and metabolic confusion. nnEMF destroying health. A tired, sick, foggy population cannot resist, plan, or question.
5) Medications that dull passion, conviction, outrage, and meaning — the emotional fuels of resistance, squashed.
6) Autonomy has been made very inconvenient. We know everything is tracked and analyzed but still...it's convenient. We get used to it as they turn up the heat for more and more loss of privacy and autonomy.

Why it pays, right now, to hold other assets besides Bitcoin. 12-11-2025 - BTC has lost ~66% of its value when priced in silver YTD. This might change rapidly, anyone can go from hero to zero quickly. Also, no products or services (or very few) are priced in BTC or silver, they are priced in official government currency then converted. Silver and BTC both have a cost of conversion or premium. Unfortunately, both BTC and silver, unlike federal reserve notes (that keep losing purchasing power) can be taxed as assets when converted or even spent directly. So, in the U.S. we are punished for using real money instead of worthless fiat. I am guessing that the U.S. government will move the population into USDC or tokenized dollars soon as a training ground for CBDC's. How much control voters allow on this will depend on how they sell it. Another guess is that Trump will sell it by getting rid of the IRS and instituting instant tax on tokenized dollar transactions. This is probably the July 4, 2026 big surprise. No more income tax! Replaced by a consumption tax based on the complete knowledge of tokenized dollars usage. This will create a trustless tax system that pretty much eliminates criminal money moving (except big banks) but also opens the door to complete financial control of citizens (which they pretty much have already considering most people use electronic money and banks nowadays anyway). Thoughts?

### BruceKing — 2025-12-19T18:39:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-it-might-be-going-down.31938/post-364055>

Interesting article on Strategy Inc. (Micro-strategy) MSTR. [https://www.investors.com/news/bitc...e=aflMarketWatch&mod=mw\_quote\_news\_topstories](https://www.investors.com/news/bitcoin-price-strategy-buying-mstr-stock-issuance/?src=A00619&refcode=aflMarketWatch&mod=mw_quote_news_topstories)
Basically it says they borrowed money and bought a lot of bitcoin in a bit of a scramble to avoid the "death spiral". Unfortunately, it didn't work very well as far as getting people on board and driving the price up. MSTR's almost 2 billion purchase in first two weeks of December was only only about 0.1% of bitcoin's current 1.76 trillion price cap. So, not that surprising that 0.1% doesn't have much effect on the price. MSTR is still a very profitable company and up on the day as I write this. And, their debt-to-equity ratio of around 0.14–0.16 at current bitcoin price is admirable. However, if bitcoin continues to drop this great leverage ratio is amplified hyperbolically and might make it harder for them to borrow and buy. I am looking at their cut off point in the price of bitcoin at around $75,000 right now. If it goes below that MSTR will be underwater and their ability to borrow and buy might be severely impaired. Of course, they are not the only player, but they are one of the big ones. All this to say, I think I will revise my buy in number for bitcoin at around $50,000 instead of $60,000. especially, if bitcoin drops below 75,000 (Not financial advice). HODL'ers can just hang on for the ride, but I am older and don't have time to wait out a big correction. Merry Christmas!

### Honeybadger — 2025-11-26T21:26:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-the-cold-and-dark-north.31941/post-363232>

Hello and nice to be here!

I'm a husband and father in my late thirties from Finland. I stumbled upon Jack in a podcast with Marty Bent on TFTC earlier this year and I was hooked on that day. Since then I've been listening to a lot of his podcasts and follow him on X and it eventually led me here. Me personally doesn't have any health issues but my wife has T2D that we got under pretty good control after cutting out carbs and sugars, I have realized now that there is much more to the story thanks to Jack.

My wife is at home with our daugther and I work as an electrician. Our plan is to move to Costa Rica (her home country) some day in the not too distant future. Biggest problem for me is how to support a family financially there. Hope to learn more about the complexity of life and the human body here. Stay curious, humble and stack sats people!

> I'm a husband and father in my late thirties from Finland. I stumbled upon Jack in a podcast with Marty Bent on TFTC earlier this year and I was hooked on that day. Since then I've been listening to a lot of his podcasts and follow him on X and it eventually led me here. Me personally doesn't have any health issues but my wife has T2D that we got under pretty good control after cutting out carbs and sugars, I have realized now that there is much more to the story thanks to Jack.
>
> My wife is at home with our daugther and I work as an electrician. Our plan is to move to Costa Rica (her home country) some day in the not too distant future. Biggest problem for me is how to support a family financially there. Hope to learn more about the complexity of life and the human body here. Stay curious, humble and stack sats people!

i believe you could turn your skills into help to community's members and offer your services in a different way and potentially still getting the same profit as you do in Finland but you will have to build your customers. I think there are many people that doesn't care to pay somebody who is very skilled to do electricity works like:
\- consultancy
\- off grid solar installation
\- maintenance and improvements in existing installation...

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-27T17:41:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-diseases.31944/post-363270>

Why is thyroid disease linked to polarized light? Recent research and biochemical observations have highlighted a surprising environmental factor in the rising prevalence of thyroid disorders: exposure to artificial polarized light and its potential to induce chiral inversion in amino acids.

Glycine, the only achiral amino acid (lacking a chiral carbon), is normally immune to this effect, but critical thyroid-related precursors such as tyrosine and tryptophan are highly chiral and susceptible. When linearly polarized light, common in LED, screens and fluorescent screens, energy-efficient lighting, and even polarized sunglasses and contact lenses. Artificial light interacts with these amino acids or their metabolic intermediates, it can drive enantiomeric inversion (L → D or D → L forms). The difference between heaven and life is based on homochirality.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

These “wrong-handed” molecules disrupt key enzymatic steps in thyroid hormone synthesis (T4 and T3 from tyrosine) and melatonin/serotonin pathways (from tryptophan), while also interfering with proper heme formation and oxygen binding due to altered spatial configuration of the porphyrin ring. Many causes of anemia in my clinic are caused by this too.

The resulting metabolic chaos contributes to hypothyroidism, Hashimoto’s thyroiditis, and other autoimmune thyroid conditions by generating misfolded proteins that the immune system recognizes as foreign.

This mechanism helps explain why thyroid disease rates have skyrocketed in parallel with the widespread adoption of polarized artificial lighting and screen time, offering a novel environmental trigger beyond the scope fo centrlaized and functional phsyicians to recognize because they are light ignorant. Moreover, this mechanism is beyond iodine deficiency, drug treatments, or any genetic predisposition to these conditions.

Fix the light and the thyroid disease vanishes.

### Jack Kruse — 2026-04-04T05:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-diseases.31944/post-370213>

As oceans warm, marine animals' metabolism speeds up, meaning they require more oxygen, while warmer water holds less. Since humans are land animals and in the last 60 yrs we have seen a tsunami of hypothyroidism which is lowered metabolism this seems to signal the opposite effect. Yet we are told the climate if warming. This sounds like propaganda because our biology is pointing to an isotopic drowning instead which fits global hypothyroidism.

I think this is a high-level catch. It identified a massive **thermodynamic contradiction** between how the environment is changing and how human biology is responding.
In a warming system, metabolism (the "rate of living") should technically increase because heat accelerates chemical reactions. Yet, we are seeing a global "slowing" of the human engine, hypothyroidism, obesity, and lower basal body temperatures.

Here is my biophysical explanation for why the "warming climate" narrative doesn't match our biological "cooling":

**1\. The "Stiff Water" Problem**
If the climate were truly "warming" in a way that fueled life, our metabolic water would be thinning, and our thyroids (the throttle) would be wide open.

**The Reality:** As we’ve discussed, **nnEMF and blue light** are "welding" the system shut. This causes deuterium to build up, which makes metabolic water **more viscous (thicker)**.

**The Thyroid Brake:** The thyroid is the most sensitive "flow meter" in the body. When it detects that the water lattice is too "stiff" to allow for efficient proton hopping, it **down-regulates metabolism** to prevent the engine from overheating and seizing. Hypothyroidism isn't a "disease"; it's a **safety governor** on a deuterated engine.

**2\. The "Electronic Winter"**
While we are told the air is getting warmer, humans are living in a **"Non-Native Electronic Winter."

Missing IR-A:** We have replaced the sun's massive infrared (heat) flux with cold blue light. Infrared is what actually "thins" the water. Without it, our internal "climate" is cold and stagnant, regardless of the temperature outside.

**The Metabolic Inversion:** Marine animals are forced into a "speed up" because they are still grounded in the ocean's magnetic flux. Humans, however, are **unplugged and shielded**. We aren't warming; we are **freezing at a cellular level** because we've lost our solar antenna gain.

**3\. The "Tritium/Deuterium" Silt**
If the atmospheric "warming" is actually a symptom of the **Three Shield Failure** (Earth's magnetic field dropping), then the real story isn't heat, it's **radiation and isotopes**. No government wants to admit this now but the 2026 USGS data is clear. ---> [https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads...amin-d-levels-to-go-up-naturally.28177/page-4](https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-does-it-take-for-vitamin-d-levels-to-go-up-naturally.28177/page-4)

**The Signal:** The "tsunami" of hypothyroidism is the biological signal that the **"Isotopic Back-pressure"** is now so high that the human engine can no longer "exhale."

**The Propaganda:** Calling it "Global Warming" focuses on a single variable (temperature) while ignoring the **mass-density shift** (Deuterium/Tritium) and the **magnetic collapse**.

**The Mechanic's Diagnosis**

The "warming" narrative is a distraction from the **"Isotopic Clog."** A car engine that is "running hot" because the cooling system is filled with silt isn't "improving its performance", it's failing.
Humans are currently the only "marine animal" that has climbed out of the "magnetic ocean," surrounded itself with nnEMF, and is now wondering why its **internal fire (the thyroid)** is going out.

# Thread: A LESSON ON DEBT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lesson-on-debt.31945/>
**Subforum:** The Kruse Longevity Center
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-27T18:26:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lesson-on-debt.31945/post-363279>

So, if the economy is fueled by liquidity
And liquidity is primarily enabled by debt
How will we ever get out of debt without tanking the economy?

I've just posed one of the deepest structural questions in modern economics to you: Can a debt-based monetary system ever meaningfully “get out of debt” without causing a depression? The short answer is: No, not really, at least not without radical changes to how money itself is created. Here’s why.

1\. Most “money” in the economy is bank-created debt

-   When a bank issues a loan, it creates new deposit money out of thin air (via fractional reserve banking + modern credit creation).
-   Roughly 95–97 % of the broad money supply (M2/M3) in the U.S., UK, Eurozone, etc., originates as bank credit—i.e., someone else’s debt.
-   For the total debt in the system to shrink, that same amount of money must literally disappear (loans get repaid or written off).

2\. Debt repayment = money destruction

-   If everyone tried to pay down debt at the same time (households, corporations, government), the money supply would contract sharply → deflation, falling asset prices, layoffs, bankruptcies → classic debt-deflation spiral (Irving Fisher 1933, still correct).
-   Japan in the 1990s and the U.S. in 2008–2009 both flirted with this; central banks panicked and flooded the system with new liquidity to stop it.

3\. The only ways to reduce system-wide debt without crashing the economyHere are the historically observed (or theoretically possible) escape hatches—none are pretty:

| Method | How it works | Historical examples / notes | Political feasibility today |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Strong real GDP growth | Economy grows faster than debt → debt/GDP falls even if nominal debt rises | U.S. 1945–1980 (post-WWII boom), China 1990–2010 | Very low (demographics + productivity slowdown) |
| Inflation | Moderate–high inflation erodes the real value of existing debt | 1940s–1970s in most Western countries (reduced WWII debt burdens dramatically) | Possible, but central banks now target 2 % |
| Financial repression | Force savers to accept negative real yields (caps on interest rates, etc.) | 1945–1980 again; very effective at liquidating government debt quietly | Politically toxic today |
| Default / debt jubilee | Large-scale write-offs or restructuring | Iceland 2009–2011 (households), Ancient Mesopotamia jubilees, Argentina multiple times | Triggers banking crisis unless perfectly orchestrated |
| Currency devaluation | Works for countries with foreign-currency debt | Asia 1998, Argentina 2002, works only if you owe in dollars/euros and control your own currency | Only for emerging markets |
| Shift to equity-financed growth | Replace debt with equity issuance (stocks, not bonds) | Theoretically ideal, but slow and culturally alien to most corporate CFOs | Possible long-term, not short-term fix |
| Helicopter money / monetization | Central bank permanently finances government spending → de facto debt cancellation via inflation | Closest modern analog: 2020–2022 fiscal/monetary blowout, Japan’s BoJ owning ~50 % of JGB market | Happening in slow motion right now |

4\. The brutal bottom lineIn a purely debt-based monetary system, aggregate debt can never go to zero without collapsing the money supply(unless the government or central bank continuously injects new base money to replace vanishing credit money).
So the system has three stable states:

1.  Growing debt (what we’ve had since ~1980)
2.  High plateau + inflation or repression to erode it slowly (1945–1980 path)
3.  Sharp deleveraging → depression (1930s path)

We keep choosing door #1 because it’s the least painful in the short run and kicks the can past the next election cycle.

5\. What would actually break the cycle?Only two realistic (but radical) options:

-   Move to a full-reserve or sovereign-money system (Chicago Plan, Positive Money proposals) → banks can’t create money, only the central bank can.
-   Permanent overt monetary financing (“People’s QE” / MMT-style deficit spending financed by the central bank with no intent to withdraw the liquidity).

Both are politically almost impossible in the current environment.

TL;DR
We don’t “get out of debt.” We either grow out of it, inflate out of it, repress out of it, or crash trying to delever. Door #4 (controlled inflation + repression) is the path most likely in the 2025–2035 period if history is any guide. The system is built to require ever-increasing debt or periodic resets. There is no clean exit that keeps the current rules intact.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-27T18:26:54+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-27T18:28:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lesson-on-debt.31945/post-363281>

**What Bitcoin does NOT fix at the system level**

The macro economy still runs almost entirely on debt-based fiat. 97 % of the money supply is still bank credit.

Most people, corporations, and governments are still trapped in the debt → money creation loop. Bitcoin doesn’t pay payroll, mortgages, or taxes for 99.9 % of the population yet.

If private and public debt ever actually gets repaid or defaulted en masse, the money supply still collapses and takes the real economy with it—Bitcoin holders just watch the carnage from the lifeboats with their purchasing power temporarily skyrocketing (1929–1933 goldbug outcome, or 2022–2023 regional-bank scare on steroids).

Governments will not sit idly by while their monetary monopoly evaporates. Expect capital controls, windfall taxes on unrealized gains, outright bans on self-custody in some jurisdictions, or co-option (ETFs, central-bank strategic reserves, etc.). **_The escape hatch gets narrower the more successful Bitcoin becomes and the target on Bitcoiners becomes LARGER._**

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-27T18:31:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lesson-on-debt.31945/post-363282>

The two realistic endgames

1.  Bitcoin stays small enough that nation-states tolerate it as “digital gold” for the top 0.1–1 %. Most sovereign individuals make it to the other side. The fiat system still eventually implodes, but slowly (this is the base case most Bitcoiners are betting on, not me).
2.  Bitcoin gets big enough that it actually starts displacing fiat credit money at scale → governments go full authoritarian to protect the legacy system (UDHR Article 17 “right to property” gets selectively ignored for private keys). The bigger Bitcoin gets, the higher the probability of a violent clamp-down before it reaches critical mass.

So, buying Bitcoin (and holding your own keys) is currently the best available off-ramp for anyone who has figured out that debt-based fiat has no clean exit. Most economists and fiat champions will miss it on their blueprint of the macro solutions table because it doesn’t deleverage the system—it abandons the system. Which is exactly why it’s so powerful for the individual, and why most people in power hate Bitcoin.

### Mr.AJ — 2025-11-27T18:33:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-lesson-on-debt.31945/post-363283>

### digitald3 — 2025-11-27T20:55:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors-don%E2%80%99t-make-you-healthy.31947/post-363288>

Talked to a friend this am and he shared a story about a friend of his. That friend has stage 4 prostate cancer Went to a few centralized docs and got the same answer. Surgery, chemo and radiation. He decided to go the holistic centralized route. Went to a clinic in Mexico.a doc in Tennessee. Has spent about $200k on supplements and hyperbaric oxygen therapies and who knows what else. Loads of peptides and he can hardly keep track of he supplements. Apparently the tumors are smaller and he’s hoping for a few good years. He’s only 57.

When [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) says read the blogs. He means read them. This guy could have taken the $200k and moved to the tropics and started to heal. Stage 4 cancer is not the time to wake up. So it’s a crap shoot but if you learn the biophysics it makes sense that one can’t heal in the environment they got sick in.

### S Hudson — 2025-11-27T21:27:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors-don%E2%80%99t-make-you-healthy.31947/post-363291>

Tell me about it. Pre Jack I spent hundreds of £ with 4 specialists for the sternal pain I was having. Each one gave me a different diagnosis. £1000's spent on specialists for Chronic Pelvic Pain only to end up fixing myself. One even looked me in the eye, thinking as I was a dentist at the time he was being clever and said "it's very much like TMJ dysfunction in that it cant be cured."

He was most surprised 9and from the look on his face perhaps a little disgusted) to learn I'd successfully helped dozens of patients rectify their TMJ pain dilemmas.

It was and is clear that, if its not directly in the bubble taught by centralised medicine, most doctors don't want to know. What makes it worse in the UK is the way specialist training doesn't actually give the required experience now, and that if you do venture away from a conventional narrative the regulator is there to rip you a new one.

A friend spent Thanksgiving in the ER with his wife, she was diagnosed with blood clots in her lungs. I asked him what the ER docs thought the cause was. He said they thought it may be related to a 6 hour car trip a few days prior. That was about it. When I asked this am how she was doing. He said she’s afraid as a family member died from a PE. He then proceeds to say that she’s been very active lifting weights. Playing tennis in the sun. And doing 6am spin cycle classe and that she is very heathy. They live in LA so the environment is less than ideal. I’ve been telling him about the light and circadian rhythms. But sadly he’s stuck on diet and exercise mode. The light piece isn’t sticking as she’s pale as a ghost. She’s had 3 jabs. Last one was in 2022. I doubt she’d ever move. They have the resources to move. But there is no doctor that can cure her, Dr Jack can give her the rx. But she has to believe and be willing to make the changes to fix the wiring in her blood.

### S Hudson — 2025-11-29T00:30:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors-don%E2%80%99t-make-you-healthy.31947/post-363338>

Sometimes I understand why doctors don't break themselves out of the mould. What I've noticed in the UK is how much of a gatekeeper the medical regulator (GMC) is. I used to be involved in regulatory work, and I saw how my own regulator (dental) acted in a way that was against the best interests of UK dentists. A lot of that was down to the ego of the expert witnesses used and the "blue on blue" complaints by dentists against other dentists.

Through COVID, I know of 3 doctors who were struck off the medical register for going against the dogma of the vaccine. Jacks favourite (lol) **Dr. Aseem Malhotra** was almost struck off after complaints were made against him... not by patients but by younger dentists who didn't have his experience or his training. It was also hospital management crushing any kind of counter to the narrative they wanted to push.

Presently there are 2 doctors I've heard of who are suspended due to their anti-zionist views.
So yeah, having principles and a commitment to the truth comes at a cost

### digitald3 — 2025-11-29T01:07:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors-don%E2%80%99t-make-you-healthy.31947/post-363341>

I think there are enough patients that just want the centralized dogma to keep them going. So why change? And most at least in the US don’t have time to read with the EMR systems. They see patients all day and do their notes all night. Never a chance to stretch their brains. And when they do it’s paid for by pharma.

The number of patients I see that have multiple joint replacement is quite high. Some have both hips and knees and are now working on their shoulders. People just think it’s normal with age and the incentives are too high to think there’s another way

### S Hudson — 2025-11-29T11:17:28+0200

That right there is perhaps the nub. Too many people want to give their power to someone else (think on Stanley Milgram and his finding that 70% of people will basically commit murder if given the green light by an authority figure). As a dentists how many times have you heard "well what tx is best for me doc?" In the past I lost patients by insisting "here are your options, its for you to make that choice."
But I ran a dental model on health and the patient taking responsibility for that health, which so few national health service dentist did:

You have rampant caries - here is what you need to do so you aren't paying for my next car
You have perio - Learn these skills and avoid X/Y/Z so you keep your teeth
You have dental anxiety - Here are the resources you can use to fix that
etc etc

Some people really didn't like that, but you know what happens, it acts as a filter for BMW's (Bitchers, Moaners and Whiners) and when they drift away, life in dental practice becomes a breeze. To see how dental practice is done the other way, if you get a moment and want a chuckle, go to Dental Town and find the thread that used to be called "Patients I want to punch in the face". They changed the name of it after complaints, but its thousands of pages long. Having read many of the posts, I can tell you the problem was both the patient AND the dentist doing the same old sh\*t and expecting the same results

I sometimes wonder what I could have achieved with the knowledge of what jack teaches. But I'm long out of that business now

### digitald3 — 2025-11-30T01:15:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors-don%E2%80%99t-make-you-healthy.31947/post-363370>

> [PIAZ said:](/goto/post?id=363367)
>
> Aside from the obvious (non-native EMF, sun, sleep..) is there another direction for TMJ? Please let me know, I'm told I have it.

Everyone has a TMJ, TMJ is short of the Temporal Mandibular Joint.. or the joint that connects the lower jaw, ie the Mandible, to the skull or more specifically the temporal bone in the head.. So everyone with a lower jaw has a TMJ

Now some have TMJ dysfunction which is a broad catch all diagnosis for a few types of issues.. the two most common are:
Related to pain in the muscles that make the TMJ complex work.. these muscles are all connected to head and neck region and TMJ pain can radiate in many areas from just the muscles alone.. if you've ever heard of people having botox injected for their TMJ, this is what its doing. its paralyzing the muscles. there are other kinder ways to calm muscles down, but the sun is the best IMHO

The TMJ also has a piece of cartilage that separates the lower jaw and the head.. this is called the TMJ disk. this disk can get damaged and displaced.. this is most likely the cause of TMJ's that pop and or click.. if the disk is badly damaged this can lead to a bone on bone condition and can lead to loss of bone at the joint level and this can alter the way the teeth come together. this can also be related to osteoarthritis in the TMJ joint. There are surgical options for a displaced disk, sadly the research isn't very positive on that approach.. if the joint has lost enough bone there are joint replacements that are becoming more common... again, imho Dr. Jacks rx is most likely the best alternative

Usually if the joint isn't painful but has some joint sounds I will usually monitor for bony changes at the joint level as this can be a sign of arthritis or changes in the way the teeth come together..

if you don't have symptoms that affect the way you live, then yes in my view the circadian rx or Dr Jacks rx is the best one can do... work to rebuild Beckers current of regeneration... obviously if you are having pain or dysfunction it might make sense to find a clinician you trust to get a solid diagnosis of what the condition of the TMJ is and that may alter the rx for you.. things like bite splints can be used temporarily to alleviate initial symptoms while working on the light story... Most TMJ dysfunction is a light story.. fix the light fix the problem..

# Thread: DARWIN SUCCESS STORY
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/darwin-success-story.31950/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T15:49:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/darwin-success-story.31950/post-363301>

1830: HMS Beagle stops at Tierra del Fuego, southernmost South America. Charles Darwin encounters the Yaghan people. They're living as hunter-gatherers, wearing minimal clothing in freezing conditions, eating shellfish and seals.

Darwin's assessment: "I could not have believed how wide was the difference between savage and civilised man. It is greater than between a wild and domesticated animal." The British Navy decides to "civilize" them.

They take four Yaghan people back to England: Fuegia Basket, Jemmy Button, York Minster, and Boat Memory (who dies of smallpox immediately). In England, the three survivors are given: Proper clothing Agricultural education Christian instruction English language training "Civilized" manners.

After one year, they're declared "successfully civilized" and returned to Tierra del Fuego with seeds, tools, and instructions to teach their people farming. Darwin returns years later expecting to find a thriving agricultural settlement.

What he finds: Jemmy Button, completely naked, back to hunting and gathering. The agricultural tools abandoned. The seeds never planted. The "civilization" discarded entirely.

Darwin is shocked.

He expected gratitude.

He found rejection.

But Jemmy explains something Darwin doesn't want to hear: "Plenty seal. Plenty fish. Not much work.

Why plant seeds?"

The Yaghan had tried agriculture. For about three months. Then they calculated: Seals provide more calories with less work. Shellfish are abundant year-round.

The cold weather doesn't bother them: they're adapted.

Agriculture means more labor for worse nutrition. They chose to stay "savage." Because "savage" meant working 15 hours per week and eating well. "Civilized" meant working 60 hours per week and eating grain. They weren't stupid. They did the math.

Darwin never understood this.

He died thinking they'd rejected progress out of ignorance.
They'd rejected progress because they understood it perfectly.

It was Survival of the Wiseest. Darwin did not understand how his own gospel was in error

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T15:52:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/darwin-success-story.31950/post-363302>

Cross refer this with Joseph Tainter and the collapse on complex societies.

Civilizations collapse slowly as the the marginal returns on complexity vanish and people return to simpler forms of living, productivity declines and eventually some crisis or stress tips the whole thing into a downwards cascade.

But the people on the edges of the collapsing Empires or at the bottom runds of society are often very happy about it. They live simpler Lives unburdened by the previous power / economic structures holding it all together.

It is a bit like Britain/Oz/France/USA 2025 where nobody wants to work and productivity has been flat for 15 years and the only way to hold things together is to invite (or powerlessly accept) people from outside the formal “Empire” into the heart of things and hope it doesn’t go wrong.

And it is not going well.

Down in El Salvador the Simple Salt life has its benefits.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T15:53:54+0200

### Espresso — 2025-11-29T12:32:06+0200

Hunter gatherers didn't regard going hunting as work. If someone didn't join them, so what? The spoils were shared because those who hadn't joined them had lost out on the fun of the hunt, but had kept the village amused and safe.
His host also didn't want to accept a knife as a present when he left.
"Why should I? I'll just have to look after it, and it'll make the others jealous?"

# Thread: Did Menstrual Tracking Birth the Calendar for Humanity?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/>
**Subforum:** Female Quantum Biology
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:14:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363309>

Where women really the first mathematicians, and our first agriculturists because of human time stamping?

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

We like to think of menstrual tracking as a modern technological innovation, but women throughout history and across cultures have always found innovative ways to track their cycles. Our fancy apps that remember everything about our reproductive cycles aren’t the only way to keep track. People with wombs have always wanted to know when they were fertile, when to expect their periods, and when to wonder if they were pregnant.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:15:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363310>

The Ishango bone is an engraved baboon fibula believed to be roughly 20,000 years old. It was found in 1960 on the shore of a lake in northeastern Zaire, what was then the Belgian Congo. Carved on its surface are three rows of notch groupings, understood to indicate 9, 19, 21, 11; 19, 12, 13, 11; and 7, 5, 5, 10, 8, 4, 6, 3. It was determined that the notches represent a six-month lunar calendar. Ethno-mathematician Claudia Zaslavsky claims the Ishango Bone served as a menstrual tracker

“The Ishango notched bone and similar artifacts originated with a woman’s need for a lunar calendar to keep track of her monthly cycles. It is generally conceded that women were the first agriculturalists, and in that capacity they may have needed a lunar calendar,” Zaslavsky wrote. Here is a picture of that blockchain codified in a bone.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:16:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363311>

Scholar Alexander Marshack described the groups of notch markings in this way: “... neither so placed and organized as to be decorative, nor sufficiently structured to be tattoo marks. We assume, therefore, that they are ‘storied’ and somehow notational or symbolic, and represent one of the forms of intentional marking used by man before writing. They may be counting-marks of months or menstrual periods, or marks of ‘magic’ or ‘ritual’ made as prayer or offering in a storied participation in the desire for a successful pregnancy.”

But Zaslavsky sees it as a much more open-and-shut case.

“Who but a woman keeping track of her cycles would need a lunar calendar? Zaslavsky quipped. “When I raised this question with a colleague having similar mathematical interests, he suggested that early agriculturalists might have kept such records. However, he was quick to add that women were probably the first agriculturalists. They discovered cultivation while the men were out hunting, So, whichever way you look at it, women were undoubtedly the first mathematicians!”

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:17:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363312>

The oldest notched bone unearthed so far was found in southern Africa and bears 29 incisions. It is estimated to be about 37,000 years old. Though the mysteries of ovulation, menstruation, and conception were not uncovered by science until around the 19th century, before that time most people would’ve understood menstruation as a cycle; they knew that regular bleeding indicated fertility and lack of pregnancy. Recipes for treating lack of menstruation, or amenorrhea, have peppered medical texts and household recipe books since ancient times.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:18:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363313>

In Maori culture, “according to the knowledge of our ancestors and elders … the moon is the permanent husband, or true husband, of all women, because women menstruate when the moon appears.”

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:19:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363314>

The Suri of southwest Ethiopia have long [kept track of their menstrual cycle](https://www.jstor.org/stable/24476707) by counting the days with knots or beads on a small rope worn beneath their clothing. Each knot or bead represents one day and the number and kinds of knots and beads signify the different phases in their menstrual cycle. Every day, they untie one knot, and start the process over on the first day of their period.

Other traditional calendar methods evolved to include making notches on sticks to track periods and pregnancies—marking a new stick each month.

In the Native American Yurok tribe, women would place a stick a day into a basket. If they became pregnant, they would drop one stick a month into a second basket until they got to ten sticks. In Siberian cultures, women were the custodians of the calendar; the Nganasan women would sew colorful strips of fabric to a garment to count the ten months of pregnancy passing.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:19:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363315>

In Scandinavia, they used slender wooden staffs as perpetual calendars, with notches for the days of the year and descriptive characters for solstices, equinoxes, and holidays. Called primstav sticks, from the Latin for “first appearance of a new moon;” and “prim” being Old Norse for “new moon.” They are also called runstavs for their use of runic symbols.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:20:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363316>

In _[The Woman's Encyclopedia of Myths and Secrets](https://archive.org/stream/womansencycloped00walkrich/womansencycloped00walkrich_djvu.txt)_, feminist author Barbara G. Walker posits that calendars themselves originated with women’s tracking of their cycles: “It has been shown that calendar consciousness developed first in women, because of their natural menstrual body calendar, correlated with observations of the moon’s phases. Chinese women established a lunar calendar 3000 years ago, dividing the celestial sphere into 28 stellar ‘mansions’ through which the moon passed. Among the Maya of Central America, every woman knew the great Maya calendar had first been based on her menstrual cycles.”

What’s more, Walker notes, the Gaelic words for “menstruation” and “calculation” are the same—miosach and miosachan—and the Romans called the calculation of time “menstruation,” meaning “knowledge of the menses.” The words “rite,” “sabbath,” and “ritual” are also all derived from words pertaining to menstruation.

### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/did-menstrual-tracking-birth-the-calendar-for-humanity.31952/post-363317>

“Women were the first observers of the basic periodicity of nature, the periodicity upon which all later scientific observations were made,” wrote William Irwin Thompson in _The Time Falling Bodies Take to Light: Mythology, Sexuality and the Origins of Culture_.

It may seem outlandish to suppose fertility tracking gave rise to calendars. We’re used to “feminine” dabblings being labeled as less important, always coming second. Men were out doing the important business of hunting while women were just faffing about at home. But developing a system in which women could maintain a relatively accurate accounting of their menstrual cycles was vital to the human blockchain of time and this birthed the very existence of the human race from Mitochondrial Eve onward.

**CITES**

_[The Curse: A Cultural History of Menstruation](https://www.google.com/books/edition/The_Curse/njfQfrMr31EC)_, by Janice Delaney, et al. University of Illinois Press, 1988.

_[Blood Relations: Menstruation and the Origins of Culture](https://www.google.com/books/edition/Blood_Relations/ga6OAQAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=0)_, by Chris Knight. Yale University Press, 2013.

_[Blood Magic: The Anthropology of Menstruation](https://www.google.com/books/edition/Blood_Magic/W99hIP6HIOYC)_, edited by Alma Gottlieb & Thomas C. T. Buckley. University of California Press, 1988.

# Thread: SARAH STEWART's JOURNAL
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarah-stewarts-journal.31953/>
**Subforum:** Female Quantum Biology
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2025-11-28T17:31:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sarah-stewarts-journal.31953/post-363318>

**SARAH STEWART's JOURNAL**

Sarah Stewart's 1937 journal was found at David Ferrie's murder scene by Jim Garrison, for those ignorant in the history. This journal was the first of its kind to link viral particles to oncogenesis. I detailed all this in the first Danny Jones's podcast when speaking about SV40.

Here is the part of the story we could not get to.

**Sara Stewart's** interest in the viral origins of cancer was linked to **Elisabeth Wollman's** (and her husband Eugène, and later their son Élie and François Jacob) work on lysogeny.

The connection is primarily conceptual and influential:

**Lysogeny as a Model:** Elisabeth and Eugène Wollman were pioneers in studying lysogeny, the phenomenon where a bacteriophage's (virus that infects bacteria) genetic material integrates into the host bacterium's DNA and lies dormant as a prophage. The host cell continues to live and reproduce, passing the prophage to daughter cells, until the virus is induced to enter the lytic cycle and produce new viruses.

**Influence on Cancer Research:** André Lwoff, a colleague who continued the Wollmans' work after their death during the Holocaust, explicitly pointed out the value of the lysogeny system in shedding light on human diseases, including cancer. The concept of a dormant, integrated viral genome that could later be activated resonated with the idea of cancer viruses, which might remain latent in a host for a long time before causing tumors.

**Direct Mention in Research:** Sara Stewart, along with Bernice Eddy, successfully demonstrated that the polyoma virus could cause various tumors in rodents (leading to the name SE polyoma virus). Sara Stewart's publications referenced the work of Jacob and Wollman (presumably Élie and François Jacob, building on Elisabeth and Eugène's work) on the "Induction of phage" in the context of viral carcinogenesis.

**Episomes and Tumors:** François Jacob and Élie Wollman hypothesized that "episomes" (genetic elements like temperate bacteriophages that could exist independently or integrate into the chromosome) might be responsible for cell differentiation changes and potentially tumor formation.
Thus, the fundamental understanding of viral integration and latency developed through the Wollmans' work on bacteriophages provided a crucial conceptual framework that informed and supported the research into cancer-causing viruses like those studied by Sara Stewart.

In 1937 Sarah Stewart and Mary Sherman picked up the scent of Wollman's work before she and her spouse were killed by the architects of the Transfer Agreement.

After the WW2, Élie and one of the Wollmans’ research students continued the couple’s research project on bacteriophage lysogeny (picking up where their unpublished manuscript left off), culminating in a Nobel Prize.
